@rfarley3 thanks a lot for your research and suggestions, that surely saved me some time!

> 0xe7, ea, f0, f1, f9: from the datasheet, these look like g, j, p, q, y (respectively) such that their tails are in the bottom 2 dot rows. I haven't verified this on my LCD to see if they are ignored or displayed raised on 5x8 dot panels. I have not found the UTF codes for bottom aligned/raised g, j, p, q, y to use in 0x67, 6a, 70, 79. But I don't know how much I care about accuracy here since encoding g as 0xe7 is unintuitive.

Yes, looks like the A00 ROM contains some full-height (10 pixel) characters. I don't own any LCD with 5x10 pixel chars, so let's simply ignore those :) People that want them should probably work with `unichr()` since there are probably no unicode codepoints defined for those characters.

> 0xe9 is superscript-neg-1, which I can't find as a single width combo-char, so I use \u207b\u00b9.
> 0xf8, it is x-bar \u0078\u0304.

Using a custom function instead of a codec means that we can support patterns like that :)

> 0xeb alternative could be superscript-x \u02e3.
> 0xed alternative could be the Pounds Sterling symbol \u00a3.
> 0xf6 alternative could be a variant of cap-sigma \u2211.
> 0xff alternative could be a taller block \u2588.
> 0xa0 Given how this is just before the kana characters, I suspect that the original mapping is actually intended to represent the Japanese space character \u3000.

Great suggestions, thanks!

> 0xde This is technically correct for decoding hitachi bytes to UTF, the character rarely doesn't modify the previous character. But I don't think this is what we want for encoding. It is one of those UTF chars that modify the previous char, such that they both only take up a single character width. On the LCD, they can't share a character and should each have its own char. The stand-alone version is \u309b. I do not know if there is a 1/2 width version of this character. My vote would be for consistency to mimic what is on the LCD, and I would decode 0xde to \u309b. But allow both \u309b and \uffdf to encode to 0xde.

Since we're dropping decoding alltogether, I'll simply add both versions to the encoder.

> 0xdf Same as L243, but for \u309c. Additionally, there is very very visually similar \u00b0.

Good point, added all three versions.

If a function evaluation fails the record should be marked as a failure via finish_failure, so what I think happens is that POAP is waiting for you to either mark the record as a success or as a failure, but that won't happen since you raised an exception that no one is going to catch. The dimension missmatch exception that is being raised in some of the test problems will be removed in the next version since the input checking is now taken care of by the strategies and the exception was never taken care of anyway.

I'm in the process of making the strategies more flexible so that they don't keep trying to evaluate the same input over and over again until the evaluation completes, but rather gives the user more power when it comes to dealing with failed evaluations.

用yutiansut和rainx  的代码修改了一个函数：
tdx_get_servers()，用途是测试ip列表中的服务器速度，返回连接速度较快的n个列表。把servers=tdx_get_servers()放在程序头部
在程序内编写涉及到api.connect时，用随机函数从该列表中取得地址：
api.connect(server[randint(1,n)],7709)
虽然没有采用多线程，但可以部分缓解对单个服务器的压力。
更进一步的数据读取可以采取多线程，要注意servers作为参数是否传递进多线程子函数。


from pytdx.hq import TdxHq_API
from datetime import datetime,timedelta
import pandas as pd
tdx_ip = [
'101.227.73.20',
'59.173.18.140',
......
]

def ping_tdx(ip):
    t1 = datetime.now()
    api = TdxHq_API()
    try:
        with api.connect(ip, 7709):
            api.get_index_bars(9, 1, '000001', 0, 100)            
        return (datetime.now() - t1).total_seconds()
    except:
        return 9999

def tdx_get_servers( n=5, df=False):
    lt = []
    for t_ip,t_s in zip(tdx_ip,map(ping_tdx,tdx_ip)):
        lt.append([t_ip,t_s])
    server_list = pd.DataFrame(lt).sort_values(by=1)
    if df:
        return server_list
    server_list = server_list[server_list[1] < 0.2]
    return list(server_list.iloc[0:n,0].values)

If I understand this thread correctly, the idea is to make `Measurements` or at least the error propagation part of it something that can be specified by the user. If so, I agree, this would be a valuable approach. Somewhere the user could specify the error propagation backend that they wanted to use. Different error-propagation tools could provide there own backends. The backend API could specify the standard calls that the `__numpy_ufunc__` or other method would call to propagate the error. Something like this:

```python
Measurement.backends.error_propagation.base.propagate_error(X, F, COV, **KW) -> DF
"""
base class for Measurement class error propagation backend. Implements the default method.
:param X: a sequence of dependent variables
:param F: function that takes X
:param COV: a covariance matrix for X
:param KW: backend specific keyword arguments
:return: DF a covariance matrix for the output of F(X)
"""
```

As it is I also fall into the camp of writing our own package, [UncertaintyWrapper](https://github.com/SunPower/UncertaintyWrapper) because we needed a much faster implementation than the autodifferentiation or Monte Carlo approaches, we also needed something that did not overload or force us to rewrite our code using specific implementations of ufuncs and most importantly we needed something that could wrap extensions in C/C++. Currently our method works with the Pint wrapper, but would be improved by PR #364.

Also, I think that use of the new explicit overloading method in Numpy-1.11 `__numpy_ufunc__`  is the correct direction to go for overloading ufuncs. There may be issues with the approach that @alexfeld describes:

> write methods for the class such as `sin` so that `numpy.sin` calls them

... which is according to these threads ([numpy-7591](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/7519), [uncertainties-47](https://github.com/lebigot/uncertainties/issues/47)) ...

> an ad hoc hack inside several survivor ufuncs.

> Also, this behavior is kinda broken and causes problems, so I hope we are able to deprecate it soon. I would recommend not trying to build new things on top of it.

> A fast better solution is the `__numpy_ufunc__` approach

alias fuck='eval $(thefuck $(fc -ln | tail -n 2 |head -n 1)); fc -R'

So, after looking at this for a small while, it appears to be a bit of an Heissenbug.

If I add an `np.random.seed(0)`, the test fails in both python 2 and 3. With the seed `999` it succeeds in both versions.

So in short, your data isn't good enough. But that is kind of funny in it's own right. Creating 100 times as much data makes it work (and wrecks your memory). An alternative fix is initializing `A = Parameter(800)`.

It sort of makes sense: 1D Gaussians are already difficult to fit to, due to a lot of local minima, let alone 2D Gaussians. This is one of the reasons visual guessing your initial parameters can be so valuable.

Hi,

Yes, this a weak point of FuseSoC. The generators are supposed to fix this, but as you have noticed, it is not implemented (there have been [some progress](https://github.com/benreynwar/fusesoc_generators) by @benreynwar lately, but it's not a general solution yet).

As you say, this is a pretty common situation. There are some cases where the scripts functionality can help out. As an example, I'm using a script to copy some extra files and generate a version register based on git tags and sha of the top-level source repository in one of my projects. The trick is to create dummy files that FuseSoC will export and then let a pre_build_script overwrite those files. This only works however if you know beforehand what the names of the files will be.

To your help you have some environment variables that can be used in the scripts
`WORK_ROOT` points to the working directory of the EDA tool (e.g. build/top_level_core/bld-tool) and `FILES_ROOT` points to the (exported) source directory of each core. There are also `BUILD_ROOT` and `SYSTEM_ROOT`, but those are only kept for backwards compatibility and will be removed at some point.

This also means that there is another limitation. Unless you run with the just recently added `--no-export` flag, there is no way to reference script files from the original repo locations. The solution here is to also list the script files in your .core file and use `file_type=user` for them. That way they will be exported, and you can reference the from the scripts section with `$FILES_ROOT/your_script.sh`

Hope this helps until the generators support is in place

So, this could be doable. In particular, I would expose this as a constructor argument for `NotebookRunner`.

However, there is a bit of a trick. In iPython 2.x, there is `kernel_cmd`, which is the command to be run to start the kernel and provide it a connection file provided via a `list`. In iPython 3.x and 4.x (not 2.x), there is still `kernel_cmd`, but it is deprecated and `kernel_name` is preferred, which takes the name of a kernel as some sort of `str`. I expect `kernel_cmd` won't survive to iPython 5.x, but I could be surprised.

Here are the options:

1. Expose both `kernel_cmd` and `kernel_name` directly.
   * Simplest solution. Works on all version of iPython currently supported.
   * Will eventually need to break API and remove `kernel_cmd`, which breaks iPython 2.x support.
2. Expose `kernel_name` directly and construct `kernel_cmd` for iPython 2.x.
   * Avoids having to break the API later.
   * Explicit about what it represents.
   * May run into weird commands used for iPython 2.x that we can't easily be reproduced.
3. Expose something like `kernel`, which is interpreted as `kernel_cmd` or `kernel_name`.
   * Avoids having to explicitly break the API later.
   * Works on all iPythons.
   * Avoids weird command construction.
   * Eventually, will need to break interpretation of argument as `kernel_cmd` at some future point, which could be thought of as breaking the API.
4. Drop iPython 2.x support and add `kernel_name`. Thinking of this ( https://github.com/paulgb/runipy/issues/78 ).
   * Avoids breaking the API.
   * Explicit about what it is.
   * No weird conversions or interpretations.
   * Breaks version compatibility.

Personally, 3 doesn't sound so bad to me for now. Thoughts?

You need the stale check to deal with numerical failures in the trust
region sub-problem solver.  They are rare but can happen for some badly
conditioned problems.

If rho <= 0 then this code should be executing over and over:
https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/ca498ac2d214fd679f8c641f90d08fb63cac11f6/dlib/optimization/optimization_trust_region.h#L304
which would lead to termination once radius gets small enough.  Which
happens quickly.  But for some reason that doesn't happen on your machine?
Are you sure the initial radius isn't zero?
The error occurs for python 3.5 as well.
No.  If you want a smaller model you will have to train your own.

I'm still not sure about doing it exactly this way.  Why not keep everything the same as it is currently except remove the compression?  This way, only programs or libraries that #include the header file will have their sizes increased.  So neither the dlib shared or static library files will have this data in it.  Also, I'm likely to add more saved models like this to dlib in the future and I don't want to keep increasing the size the shared library file, which is what would happen if the data was in a .cpp file each time.

Looking at the situation "a", I think this query should return  just `sys.UpTime.0` if this object exists at the agent or nothing e.g. empty list of var-binds. At least this is how it is supposed to behave.

With situation "b" you should get the whole MIB, that's right. I'm interested to learn if `sysUpTime.0` object is among the returned set?

Note that when you request `sysUpTime`, the likely *next* object is `sysUpTime.0`. If it is absent at the agent, the next object might be `sysContact.0` which belongs to a different OID subtree than `sysUpTime` therefore it should not be returned. This is exactly what the condition you dropped is all about.

To figure out why `GETNEXT sysUpTime` does not return `sysUpTime.0` we probably need to enable pysnmp debugging and look what's coming from your agent:

    from pysnmp import debug
    debug.setLogger(debug.Debug('all'))

Hi, reporting back on this. I put together a simple example on how Spark and durable_rules can work together. In this example a rule looks for two words that contain Spark and Python. The words resulting from the file aggregation are posted to the ruleset. 

```python
from __future__ import print_function
import sys
from operator import add
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from durable.lang import *

# ruleset definition
with ruleset('words'):

    @when_all(c.first << m.word.matches('.*Spark.*'),
              c.second << m.word.matches('.*Python.*'))
    def spark_and_python(c):
        print('Triggered rule')
        print('   -> {0}'.format(c.first.word))
        print('   -> {0}'.format(c.second.word))

# host creation requires a redis instance
host = create_host([{'host': 'test.8zj18v.0001.usw2.cache.amazonaws.com', 'port': 6379}])
host.run()

# spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("PythonWordCount").getOrCreate()

# counting words
lines = spark.read.text(sys.argv[1]).rdd.map(lambda r: r[0])
counts = lines.flatMap(lambda x: x.split(' ')) \
              .map(lambda x: (x, 1)) \
              .reduceByKey(add)
output = counts.collect()

# looking at results
for (word, count) in output:
    host.post('words', {'word': word})

spark.stop()
```

Anyway, you seem to suggest going back to previous approach where `physical` and `geometrical` were dumped in gmsh files as integer tags, and not as now in the `$ElementData` section. I'm certainly not opposed to that, I already said that IMHO the current behavior is a regression. However, maybe I would suggest to somehow namespace these special cell data names, let say something like `gmsh:physical` and `gmsh:geometrical` (or maybe `region:physical` to make it more format-independent), so now the gmsh writer can have some confidence that the data is actually meant to be dumped as region tags. With a bit of namespacing on the keys for cell data, I think you can go back to the original approach of using `cell_data`, and forget about a special  treatment for regions.

IMHO, your current writer misunderstand the intention of `$NodeData` and `$ElementData` sections. These sections are supposed to attach scalar or field data associated to nodes or cells, they have nothing to do with specifying the physical and elementary regions each entity is tagged with.

this looks like it's working as expected. The wizard closes, then the application quits because all windows attached to the application are closed, but the asyncio loop attached to the QApplication hasn't closed yet. You either need the extra QWidget or you need to reorganize your application.

Example reordering:

```python
async def amain():
    # if these two lines are uncommented you'll be able to see both wizards
    # b = QPushButton('hi')
    # b.show()
    wiz1 = QWizard()
    wiz1.addPage(QWizardPage())
    wiz1.setWindowTitle("First Wizard")
    result = await async_dialog_exec(wiz1)
    if result != QDialog.Accepted:
        return

    wiz2 = QWizard()
    wiz2.addPage(QWizardPage())
    wiz2.setWindowTitle("Second Wizard")
    result = await async_dialog_exec(wiz2)
    if result != QDialog.Accepted:
        return
   app.quit()

app = QApplication(sys.argv)
loop = QEventLoop(app)
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
with loop:
    loop.ensure_future(amain())
    loop.run_forever()
```

Just because this is expected doesn't mean it's the best way for quamash to be designed. (perhaps the loop should wrap a `QtCore.QEventLoop` or `QtCore.QThread` instead of a `QApplication` in order to keep app from quitting until the future run with `run_until_complete` exits

@maximumG Some feedback on this:

You should create an `autodetect` method in base_connection.py and eliminate your code duplication.

Move this to base_connection.py and then in the child drivers just call the parent class and pass in the search strings.

```
+    @staticmethod
 +    def autodetect(session):
 +        """
 +        """
 +        matches = ["Cisco IOS Software", "Cisco Internetwork Operating System Software"]
 +        try:
 +            response = session.send_command("show version | inc Cisco")
 +            for m in matches:
 +                if m in response:
 +                    return 99
 +        except:
 +            return 0
 +        return 0
```

So I would think the definition of autodetect in base_connection.py should be:

```
def autodetect(self, matches, default_priority=99):
```

Not sure what you want the default_priority to be on a success.

Also, we should make this be a normal method and not a static method (since the `session` is already bound to the object).

I would also vote, we don't do a bare except so at a minimum `except Exception` (or better would be to specify the exceptions we are looking for. Note, it is probably better to get some exceptions here (otherwise we are probably hiding errors by autodetect just returning 0).

from PIL import Image

    api.Recognize()
    ri = api.GetIterator()
    for r in iterate_level(ri, RIL.WORD):
        # rectangle
        print r.BoundingBox(RIL.WORD)
        # text
        print r.GetUTF8Text(RIL.WORD)
```

> OK, then as I said, if one of the tasks commonly uses a *sv format with quote characters, we should support that. Is this the case?

picking this one up again..

In general, *sv files do use quote characters, and no, they're not generally consistent.  This is part of the enormous headache of writing a csv parser.  I wouldn't say that it's exactly "common" for any particular task, but I'd expect it to happen more often for tasks like segmentation, where the label space can be open and have strings with spaces.

The way I see it, we have two reasonable options here:

* add a dependency to handle parsing sanely (eg, pandas)
* do a simple best-effort implementation (what we currently have) and otherwise punt upstream to the user.

My personal preference is to offload to pandas, since it's a pretty standard package at this point.  But I understand if others don't want to add the dependency.

May be useful for someone...Our Devstack also failed because of this bug. We just downgraded pycparser to 2.13 in requirement.txt and disabled 'tempest' service. After this, our Devstack installation was successful.
I don't know why pycparser *needs* binary wheels. And sorry about the breakage - sometimes I forget that transitively lots of stuff depends on pycparser.

Pickling was addressed in https://github.com/eliben/pycparser/issues/82 -- feel free to submit the PR for that.

As for extending nodes vs. `__slots__`, that's a good question, and it's a tradeoff. `__slots__` does make pycparser consume dramatically less memory, and I don't think PyPy would help here. I haven't had any complaints about the limitation of slots until now; on the other hand the memory/perf reduction is important for a number of downstream projects.

Since all the slots that you care about are in generated nodes, all you really need to modify is `_ast_gen.py` to give you nodes without slots. I can accept a patch that will add a command-line flag to `_ast_gen.py` making it optionally omit slots - how does that sound?

Well, this turns out to be a slightly more interesting issue than I'd expected.

The core issue is that the Python 3 `instancemethod` type isn't hashable. `instancemethod` is a type used exclusively by non-core C extensions to define instance methods, and for some reason the `instancemethod_hash` function is commented out: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/c30098c8c6014f3340a369a31df9c74bdbacc269/Objects/classobject.c#L569

There's a bit more discussion here: http://stackoverflow.com/q/40876368/71522

I've got this fixed in ff87c57!

To summarize, the decision was made to use the ``video/x-youtube``mimetype because most people map this type to youtube-dl/mpv/vlc, and those applications (at least newer versions) support opening v.redd.it links. I just tested a bunch of v.redd.it links on my laptop and confirmed that mpv still works for me, even without needing @woorst 's updated mailcap configuration. 

If you need more granularity, you could work around this by writing a custom shell script to check if the URL is v.redd.it,  and then redirect to the appropriate program yourself. E.g.

```
video/x-youtube; my_opener.sh '%'; test=test -n '$DISPLAY"

Back on the chair (and not on my mobile). In a quick search, Linux have a limit of 4096 chars for paths and 255 for filenames (in EXT4, let's take it as an example). So, running something like _this_ is really interesting:

```python
import binascii
import os
from pathlib import Path


def main():
    n = 16 * 512  # let's have no mercy, it will end up being ~16k
    randstr = str(binascii.hexlify(os.urandom(n)))
    print('the len of randstr is {}'.format(len(randstr)))

    print('\ntest using Path ...')
    p = Path(randstr)
    try:
        if p.is_file():
            print('p is a file!')
        else:
            print('p is not a file!')
    except OSError:
        print('this print should appear on your screen')

    print('\ntest using os module ...')
    if os.path.isfile(randstr):
        print('randstr is a file!')
    else:
        print('randstr is not a file and will not raise an exception')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # run as is
    main()
```

The result also:

```
the len of randstr is 16387

test using Path ...
this print should appear on your screen

test using os module ...
randstr is not a file and will not raise an exception
```

So ... I will propose a third solution: use `os.path.isfile`. At least it doesn't requires us to use regex or deal with exceptions:

```python
def load_file_or_str(path_or_str):
    if isinstance(path_or_str, Path) and \
            os.path.is_file(str(path_or_str)):
        logger.debug('reading file \"{}\"'.format(str(path_or_str)))
        return path_or_str.read_text()
    elif isinstance(path_or_str, str):
        if os.path.isfile(path_or_str):
            return Path(path_or_str).read_text()
    return path_or_str
```

What concerns me is:

- `path_or_str is None`
- `Path(None)`
- `path_or_str == ''`
- `if isinstance(path_or_str, Path) and not os.path.is_file(str(path_or_str)):`

Should we tackle this with simple checks?

Got it working - but I've caught a couple of things

Your "cert=" call is actually enabling client certificate validation - which isn't used/supported by graylog-server until 1.2 (apparently - just read this yesterday). So it's not really needed in "traditional" mode where you just want the client to connect to the server. Only the "ca*" variables and "cert_reqs" are related to server-only validation

99.9% of graylog-server users would get a cert validation error using pygelf - as typically the server is self-signed. It's always best practice to default to "secure mode" - so your code is fine, but perhaps it would be worth documenting how to disable server cert validation for those who don't care (ie they want to use TLS as a *privacy* measure instead of a *security* measure)

I'm not a python coder, so I actually couldn't figure out how to properly make pygelf disable cert validation - I ended up hacking tls.py to change to cert_reqs=ssl.CERT_NONE to do that - but I assume I should have been able to do that from my script instead? (and strace then showed that the "cert=" file was never opened - verifying my above statement about client certs :-)

However, it still works! Awesome :-)

So, if you run `SELECT * FROM [dbo].[temperature] ORDER BY [recorded]`, what do you get?

In SQL, unless you specify an ORDER BY clause, the records you get back from the database can be provided in any order, and that order can be different each time you retrieve even the same records.  If you want the records in a specific order, you have to indicate that within the ORDER BY clause.

My guess is that's a bug in bitcore.io. The Bitcoin VM won't validate signatures out of order (at least not one that's written according to the specification). From https://bitcoin.org/en/developer-reference#term-op-checkmultisig:

>:warning:  **[`OP_CHECKMULTISIG`](https://bitcoin.org/en/developer-reference#term-op-checkmultisig) warning**: The multisig verification process described above requires that signatures in the signature script be provided in the same order as their corresponding public keys in the pubkey script or redeem script. For example, the following combined signature and pubkey script will produce the stack and comparisons shown:
>```
OP_0 <A sig> <B sig> OP_2 <A pubkey> <B pubkey> <C pubkey> OP_3

Sig Stack       Pubkey Stack  (Actually a single stack)
---------       ------------
B sig           C pubkey
A sig           B pubkey
OP_0            A pubkey

1. B sig compared to C pubkey (no match)
2. B sig compared to B pubkey (match #1)
3. A sig compared to A pubkey (match #2)

Success: two matches found
```
>
>But reversing the order of the signatures with everything else the same will fail, as shown below:
>
>```
OP_0 <B sig> <A sig> OP_2 <A pubkey> <B pubkey> <C pubkey> OP_3

Sig Stack       Pubkey Stack  (Actually a single stack)
---------       ------------
A sig           C pubkey
B sig           B pubkey
OP_0            A pubkey

1. A sig compared to C pubkey (no match)
2. A sig compared to B pubkey (no match)

Failure, aborted: two signature matches required but none found so
                  far, and there's only one pubkey remaining
```

First off, I'm on Linux, but have a virtual machine for Windows. That said, I'm not familiar with Windows development, so this is just my totally naive understanding of how to get things done and not "best practices" or anything like that. Using python3.4 & python3.5, I was able to get python-twitter working as follows.

On a clean install of Windows 8 (sorry - I don't have Windows 10), these are the steps I took. I've split this up into using either Cygwin or the official Python 3.5.1 installer and then the common steps to use python-twitter after that.

#### With Cygwin
1. Install cygwin
2. Install packages for ``python3`` + ``python3-setuptools``
3. Since you'll need pip to install dependencies: download https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/raw/bootstrap/ez_setup.py
4. Run ``python3 ez_setup.py``
5. Run ``easy_install-3.4 pip``
6. Open up the cygwin terminal.


#### With Python 3.5.1 Official Binaries
1. Install Python 3.5.1 from https://www.python.org/downloads/release/python-351/ using "Add Python 3.5 to PATH"
2. Using the defaults, this install pip so there's no need to go through the other steps above.
3. Open up ``command.exe``


### Common steps
1. Clone master branch of repo (``git clone https://github.com/bear/python-twitter``)
3. ``cd`` into the cloned repository (i.e., for me, it was ``cd C:\cygwin\home\Jeremy\python-twitter``)
2. Run ``pip install -r requirements.txt``
3. Here's the program I'm testing (``test.py``):

    ``` python
import twitter

CONSUMER_KEY = 'www'
CONSUMER_SECRET = 'xxx'
ACCESS_TOKEN = 'yyy'
ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET = 'zzz'

api = twitter.Api(
    consumer_key=CONSUMER_KEY,
    consumer_secret=CONSUMER_SECRET,
    access_token_key=ACCESS_TOKEN,
    access_token_secret=ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET)

print(api.VerifyCredentials())
    ```

5. Run that program with ``python3 test.py``

--------

If you have proper access tokens, you should see the output from Twitter. Since your error output above is referencing 
```
\Python35\lib\site-packages\twitter__init__.py
```
I imagine that you've got an old installation of python-twitter lying around somewhere. I'd uninstall that (``pip uninstall python-twitter``) before trying the steps above.

Since you said that you had a workaround with tweepy, I'm going to close this issue. If you try the instructions above and have any troubles, please reopen the issue and I'll see what I missed something.

The logic:
  - 1st data inserted in the system is the master clock
  - 2nd and posterior datas will not deliver data if their current datetime is `>` (greater than) the datetime of the master

The system can neither know nor understand why a gap in the data is there. Example:
  - Datas with different timeframes like 60 minutes and days.

The "days" data doesn't have a gap. It is simply that the data is not yet ready, whist the "60 minutes" data keeps on producing bars. And the "days" data cannot/must not kick the strategy with an empty bar, because that would be an illusion and would actually break the calculation of the indicators.

Should the "Stochastic" also understand if an empty bar (once in which the values have been filled with "Nan") is such an empty bar and discard it or use the "NaN" (and disrupt the end result)? Indicators must remain simple and stupid and do a good job.

Your issue is one of "data filling". This is something I already started considering and the following classes are available in the source code:

  - `DataFiller` which was the 1st implementation. I should actually delete it, because it has been superseded by the "filter" concept.

  - `SessionFiller` which is the 2nd implementation and uses the "filter concept".
     It is only implemented for a session, because I consider unlikely that "daily" and larger timeframes have missing data. If a day is missing, it is because no trade happened that day, and that's not missing data, it is actually relevant.

To apply a filter to a data:

```python
data = btfees.MyDataFeed(.....)

data.addfilter(filter, *args, **kwargs)
```

The only 2 available "filters" so far are:
  - SessionFiller (fill missing intraday bars)
  - SessionFilter (cap the data to the given session times, which can for example filter out non-Regular Trading Hours data)

Hi,

Sorry for the late response.

I have tested your application, and it does indeed crash for me with the message `short format requires (-0x7fff - 1) <= number <= 0x7fff` when running under *XWayland*. This is caused by by the values passed to `self.mouse.move`: *Xlib*  requires *short integers*, that is signed 16 bit values.

Now, whether this should be handled by *pynput* is a difficult question---one solution would be to wrap the error in a generic `ValueError` and mark the method as raising that particular exception, another would be to cap the input values.

The root cause, however, is in your algorithm. `pynput.mouse.Listener.on_move` will be called when you call `self.mouse.move`, thus triggering another call to move the pointer, eventually leading to an overflow. You will need to react only to user triggered move callbacks.

```
result = shell.run(["sh", "-c", "java -jar "], cwd="/tmp/")
result = shell.run(["sh", "-c", "ls -lrt"], cwd="/tmp/results")
```

However, `cwd` isn't supported on minimal shells. Do you need to use a minimal shell type? If so, then you'll need to include the appropriate `cd` command in the same program as the actual command you want to run:

```
result = shell.run(["sh", "-c", "cd /tmp/; java -jar "])
result = shell.run(["sh", "-c", "cd /tmp/results; ls -lrt"])
```

Thanks for the bug report.

This looks like it would be an issue with pyang, rather than specifically with pyangbind. The problem is that when you deviate out the `subcomponent` list, then it no longer exists in the pyang schema to deviate out `name`. 

This notwithstanding, this is a buggy set of deviations, since it's never allowed to deviate out `name` since it's the key of the list.

I think you'll get the behaviour that you're expecting by simply removing the second `deviate` statement, which works in my environment.

Please take a look and come back to us if there's a pyangbind related issue here!

I had a look at the switchMap implementations of RxJava and RxJS, but I think looking at Rx for reference is not too useful. The issue here is specific to how `asyncio.wait` can return multiple completed results, `select` style. The Rx implementations use a `next`/`onNext` method that processes one item of one observable, if some `select` lurks down there somewhere, the switch operator does not get to see that both items really arrived at the "same time".

I'd advocate for yielding; imagine that streamer's and substreamer's `anext` always completed together. Without yielding, the `switch` would never yield anything, waiting for the now-current new substreamer.

Is there anything else to be decided before implementing* this?

* i.e. copying your code, adding the same cleanup as in #8, adding tests
More generally perhaps everything that is not a HMAC is public-key, so the right test is probably `if not algorithm.startswith('HS')`.

You should be able to do what you want as follows:

```python

import xlsxwriter

workbook    = xlsxwriter.Workbook('hyperlink.xlsx')
worksheet   = workbook.add_worksheet()

url_format2 = workbook.add_format({'bold': True, 
                                   'underline': 1, 
                                   'theme': 10, 
                                   'hyperlink': True})

worksheet.write_url('A1', 'http://www.python.org/')
worksheet.write_url('A3', 'http://www.python.org/', url_format2)

workbook.close()

```

Note, that you will need to set `underline`, `theme` and `hyperlink` (even if the last 2 aren't public).

Hi, you might want to try changing the cache that Breathe uses. By default it just stuffs all the results of parsing files into a dictionary which is created here: 

https://github.com/michaeljones/breathe/blob/master/breathe/parser/__init__.py#L99

The cache was introduced to speed things up but realistically it might get very big. If you replace that dictionary with an object that has a dictionary interface but never stores any data then you might have luck reducing the memory usage. I've taken some time to look into the way versioning works.
Buildout uses pkg_resources Requirement and works in the same way as
easy_install and pip for versioning.
The version can contain the following: <=, >=, ==, !=, < and >.
django>1.3, django<1.4, django!=1.3 are all valid ways to specify the version.
Unfortunately it's not possible to specify a version range.
 So, there is no way to specify the current 1.3.X release branch, except for specifying <1.4.

However the version is mostly used to pin the version. It's up to the developer to test 
a new version and use the version pin to upgrade.

Crap, sorry about that! I did not mean for my tone to be so dismissive, please forgive me and help me understand the situation a little bit better.

As I understand it, `Access-Control-Request-Headers` is only used during the preflight phase of a request. The w3c spec says that if every element in this header is not found in our list of allowed headers, we should abort, and not send CORS headers.

Today, we ignore this header, and always send the list of allowed headers with every preflight request. 

Is it possible you are not setting the `allow_headers` parameter to Flask-Cors?

@mehdi-erroussafi Ok, that didn't work out as planned. Other parts of the code depends on `_meeting_timezone` being available.

The reason we are parsing calendar item fields is that an inbox folder can contain many item types - messages, tasks, calendar items etc. When you run `account.inbox.all()` we as the server to give us all fields for all items in the inbox folder. That includes asking for calendar item fields even though your inbox may only contain messages.

Sa you can see in https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/8adaee98f6227aa0de199a362d8bb9c96e51cf57/exchangelib/items.py#L571 calendar items have three different timezone fields. `_meeting_timezone` is documented as supported only in Exchange 2007, while the other two are supported from Exchange 2010 and up. It's possible your Exchange 2007 server supports the `_start_timezone` and `_end_timezone` fields after all, for some reason.

There isn't a simple way to change the way timezone fields are selected depending on the version, but you can avoid fetching the timezone fields by using `account.inbox.all().only('subject', 'body', 'datetime_received', ...)` to request only the fields you need.

To flesh out the background a little more, this is an issue that we never encounter in local development, but which shows up in deployment after a certain amount of traffic at a certain level.  We thought it was caused by some code that I wrote that dug information out of the spec at unexpected times, and when we took that out we thought it was fixed (the errors didn't show up within the expected time period), but it turned out we had just extended the time required for the error to show up. This seems to support the notion that it's a somewhat hard-to-reach race condition (though not that hard to reach—this service isn't very high traffic, in the grand scheme of things).

After we first see this error, it's unrecoverable without restarting the server (apache 2.2/mod_wsgi), which supports the notion that there's a shared object that gets into a bad state and stays that way more or less permanently.

Different devices report the battery state differently. Shutter-contacts for example often have `LOWBAT` set to `True` if the battery is low. Thermostats however don't do that. Instead they report the remaining power. If your Thermostats battery is dead at a value of 2.1, then your automation has to monitor that value and fire an automation as soon as the value drops below 2.2.

The UNREACH is a totally different topic. This may not be properly implemented because it had a low priority. Ideally devices are never unreachable, so this hasn't received much attention.

> In Redis 2.6 or older the command returns -1 if the key does not exist or if the key exist but has no associated expire.
>Starting with Redis 2.8 the return value in case of error changed:
>
>    - The command returns -2 if the key does not exist.
>    - The command returns -1 if the key exists but has no associated expire.

So the error might be raised not only in case, when a key has no expiration time, but also when it doesn't exist.

```python

        OrderBookState = namedtuple('OrderBookState', ['orderbook', 'timestamp', 'is_snapshot'])

	def _on_message(self, msg):
		if 'e' in msg and msg['e'] == 'error':
			LOGGER.error("Websocket error: " + str(msg['m']))
			return

		usd_pair = msg['stream'].split('@')[0].upper()
		if usd_pair not in self.order_book_states:
			order_book = self._get_orderbook_snapshot(usd_pair)
			LOGGER.info("[Subscription][{}]: Init Order Book snapshot received.".format(usd_pair))
			self.order_book_states[usd_pair] = OrderBookState(order_book, timestamp=time.time(), is_snapshot=True)
		is_snapshot = self.order_book_states[usd_pair].is_snapshot
		order_book = self.order_book_states[usd_pair].orderbook
		last_update_id = order_book.get_update_id()
		payload = msg['data']
		if (is_snapshot and payload['u'] <= last_update_id) or payload['e'] != 'depthUpdate':
			return

		# https://github.com/binance-exchange/binance-official-api-docs/blob/master/web-socket-streams.md
		# The first processed should have U <= lastUpdateId+1 AND u >= lastUpdateId+1
		# While listening to the stream, each new event's U should be equal to the previous event's u+1
		if (is_snapshot and payload['U'] <= last_update_id + 1 and payload['u'] >= last_update_id + 1) or (
						payload['U'] == last_update_id + 1):
			for bid in payload['b']:
				price = Decimal(bid[0])
				quantity = Decimal(bid[1])
				if quantity == 0:
					order_book.delete_bid_order(price)
				else:
					order_book.set_bid_order(price=price, quantity=quantity)
			for ask in payload['a']:
				price = Decimal(ask[0])
				quantity = Decimal(ask[1])
				if quantity == 0:
					order_book.delete_ask_order(price)
				else:
					order_book.set_ask_order(price=price, quantity=quantity)
			order_book.set_update_id(payload['u'])
			self.order_book_states[usd_pair] = OrderBookState(order_book, is_snapshot=False, timestamp=time.time())

	def start_feed(self):
		streams = []
		for currency in self.currency_list:
			usd_pair = self.supported()[(currency, Currency.USD)]
			if usd_pair is not None:
				stream = usd_pair.lower() + "@depth"
				streams.append(stream)
				self.order_book_states[usd_pair] = OrderBookState(self._get_orderbook_snapshot(usd_pair), timestamp=time.time(), is_snapshot=True)
				LOGGER.info("[Subscription][{}]: Init Order Book snapshot received.".format(usd_pair))
		self.bm = BinanceSocketManager(self.client)
		self.bm.start_multiplex_socket(streams, self._on_message)
		self.bm.start()

	def close_feed(self):
		self.bm.close()

       	def get_current_orderbook(self, currency):
		if currency in self.cached_orderbook:
			return self.cached_orderbook[currency]
		if (currency, Currency.USD) not in self.supported():
			return
		usd_pair = self.supported()[(currency, Currency.USD)]
		order_book_state = copy(self.order_book_states[usd_pair])
		time_delay = time.time() - order_book_state.timestamp
		if time_delay > 3:
			LOGGER.warning("The {} order book is out of sync for {} second(s)".format(currency, time_delay))
			return
		return order_book_state.orderbook
	def reconnect(self):
	        self.close_feed()
		self.start_feed()
```

@jerzyk

>  if you have DB exposed, than you have more problems than exposed tokens, imho

That argument doesn't make sense given `django-rest-knox`'s explicit decision to store a hash of the token in the DB and not the plain text token as the default `rest_framework.authtoken` does.  And this decision comes wit a [significant trade-off](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/authentication.py#L193).  From  `django-rest-knox`'s own `README.rst`:

> DRF tokens are stored unencrypted in the database. This would
> allow an attacker unrestricted access to an account with a
> token if the database were compromised.
> 
> Knox tokens are only stored in an encrypted form. Even if the
> database were somehow stolen, an attacker would not be able to
> log in with the stolen credentials.

Regarding:

> I really do not understand this use case

The use case is API key/token management UIs so a user can manage the keys/tokens/secrets that other external/3rd-party applications use to access your application with that user's access rights.  This use case is common in the wild:  Google, AWS, etc..

I can describe what problem I was trying to solve.
we use pytest-junitxml plugin to generate test report, this report also include captured stdout & stderr as <system-out> & <system-err> elements.
this report is collected by jenkins-junit plugin and visualized in Jenkins UI.

my problem is that pytest-junitxml plugin doesn't include 'log' section captured by this plugin into xml.
so then our QA/Dev department can not see these logs in jenkins report.

I was considering 3 ways how to make them visible in jenkins report.

1) patch pytest-junitxml plugin, I wanted to add option what report-section to include in result.
problem is that when I add <system-log> element, it fails on xsd validation.
so alternatively I could append it to <system-out> element, but I think this logic doesn't fit into this plugin. that led me to second option.

2) patch pytest-catchlog plugin to report logs in specific report-section, the thing was rename 'log' to 'stdout' / 'stderr' and it will get included in <system-out> element and then displayed in jenkins results.

3) go with 1) and create XSLT script which merge <system-out> with <system-log> together.

this patch is the implementation of option 2).
I hope I put some light on that, I am also open to any suggestion how to do it better.

I'm curious - what was the motivation for this?

A long, long time ago [in a galaxy far away] PHPBB included its version number in the footer at the bottom of it's pages. Then arose a character [aligned with the dark side], who wrote a novel piece of malware that would infect vulnerable versions of PHPBB by automatically Googling for the vulnerable version strings, and then attack them [with lightsabers]. Eventually [the prophecy of] a more secure PHPBB version came to pass, which removed its version string from the footer and fixed the exploit, and the malware was stopped [by a well-aimed shot into one of its exhaust ports].

So, if an attacker can get the version of watchman, they can limit their known range of versions of Django (eg: version ```x.y.z``` of watchman only supports version ```a.b.c``` through ```d.e.f``` of Django). Given this information, an attacker can refine their attack strategy, allowing them to attempt fewer exploits than they would otherwise. That, in turn, makes it easier to evade IDS systems.

Basically: version information disclosure to malicious parties violates defense-in-depth guidelines.

Now, this is only an issue when either of the following is true:

1. there is no authentication done on the ```status``` or ```dashboard``` endpoints
2. a malicious party has access to the ```status``` or ```dashboard``` endpoints

The first is unlikely yet possible with PR #30 if the authentication/authorization decorator is flawed, and the second is a misplaced trust issue to being with.

There are also a few ameliorating factors:

1. Python is leaps and bounds more readable and debuggable than PHP.
2. Django is leaps and bounds more readable, debuggable, and secure than PHPBB.
3. PHP sucks. Python rocks.
4. PHP developers tend to suck.
5. Exactly 0% of this project is written in PHP.
6. Did I mention that PHP is a terrible, bug-ridden, exploit-prone language that makes it difficult to understand due to its minutiae and impossible with which to write secure code from the outset?

Given all of that, this isn't a huge deal, I just want to recognize that this PR creates a small, mild defense-in-depth violation for essentially what are misconfigured systems already. Making a note about this issue in the documentation for watchman might be a good idea to help users avoid this potential pitfall. But I think "secure by default" should be the accepted practice, so I would also suggest turning off this feature by default, and letting users turn it on if they wish.

I know this is closed. However I discovered it whilst searching for answers for myself.

userId can be used, it's unique and consistent across all devices on that account, it should not however be used for store important data as it can be reset.

For storing of important data (persistently) you need to link their account, and have your own OAuth setup.

https://developers.google.com/actions/identity/user-info

However, like a cookie, users can reset this identifier, so don't store important user data keyed off this identifier, because once it's reset, that information is no longer accessible by the user. The following list describes how the user ID behaves:

**Voice-activated speakers**
- When a voice matches - If a voice matches a registered voice profile on the device, a consistent user ID is returned. Each registered user has a different and consistent user ID that is associated with any conversation they have with the device.
- When no voice matches: If a registered user's voice isn't recognized by the device or no registered voice exists, then a different ID is used that is unique for just that conversation.

**Android mobile devices** - The user ID corresponds to the active Google account in the Google app and stays the same. The user ID on Android devices will also be the same as the user ID on a voice-activated speaker if all these cases are met:
- The user's Google account is the same on both devices.
- The user registered their voice on voice-activated speakers.
- The user's voice is matched on the voice-activated speaker.

The issue is basically that "save as new" is an add option that takes place from the change_form template. When there is an error saving as new (in this case a duplicate value on a unique field), the error is rendered on the change_form template, but there is no object_id like we might expect.

Django avoids this crash with the history button by saving the url as a variable which seems to set it to an empty string in an error case, so the history button leads to the relative path `""` in a similar "save as new" error.

Unfortunately, django passes the original `object_id` into the `change_view` that django-object-actions overwrites, but only sets the object_id to None inside its own `changeform_view` after https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/admin/options.py#L1403

The similar handling in django's `changeform_view` makes a good case for the above change. Another few options to continue the discussion:

1. Overwrite `render_change_form` instead of `change_view` so we can intercept the context after it has the correctly blank `object_id`

```
# django_object_actions/utils.py
class BaseDjangoObjectActions(object):
    ...
    def render_change_form(self, request, context, add, change, obj, form_url)
        context['tools_view_name'] = self.tools_view_name
        if add:
             context['objectactions'] = [
                self._get_tool_dict(action) for action in
                self.get_change_actions(request, obj.pk, form_url)
             ]
        else:
             context['objectactions'] = []
        return super(BaseDjangoObjectActions, self).render_change_form(
            request, context, add, change, obj, form_url)

I get that you are talking about raw engineio, but there is no great reason to use engineio alone. And even if you do that, there is already a way for an application to access `environ`, as it gets it in the connect event handler, where it can store it for later use.

The idea of passing the environments for every request that the client sends does not really go well with the way the Socket.IO protocol works. At any given time, a client using long-polling could have up to two outstanding requests, a GET request to read, and a POST request to write. In this case there could be two different `environ` dictionaries, so which one do you send to handlers? And then when the connection migrates to websocket, there are no more requests, so what do you use then, given that the only environment that you have is now stale?

The codebase have 3 point where a `DynamicFixture` object are initialized:
* in __init__.py `fixture` function
* in `DynamicFixture._process_foreign_key`method
* in `DynamicFixture._create_manytomany_relationship` method

the problem without the PR is that only in the first case use the `DDF_IGNORE_FIELDS` setting so with my PR i ensure that any call of DynamicFixture() will use the generic `ignore_fields`.

BTW the solution proposed in `#85` is wrong because by ignoring `*_ptr` it does not return error on `G()` call but the created obj is not valid!
When you try to retrieve from db the new obj it will fail.
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/db/models/#multi-table-inheritance 
```Python
# settings
DDF_IGNORE_FIELDS = ['*_ptr']

# model.py
class ModelA(PolymorphicModel):
    ...

class ModelB(ModelA):
    ...

class ModelC(models.Model):
    a = FK(ModelB)

# test.py
obj = G(ModelB)
ModelB.objects.get(pk=obj.pk) # <-- Here django throw error because he cannot find a valid parent instance of ModelA relative the created obj
```
The real solution to fix the integration with `django-polymorphic` is to avoid creation of `polymorphic_ctype` field with this code:
```Python
DDF_IGNORE_FIELDS = ['polymorphic_ctype']
```
and modify `set_data_for_a_field` method to handle an AttributeError raised by `*_ptr` fields.

Bring discussion to the correct place. I've implemented an ndarray version of F1 score when doing the experiments and this is my code
```python
def nd_f1(pred, label, num_class, average="micro"):
    """Evaluate F1 using mx.nd.NDArray

    Parameters
    ----------
    pred : nd.NDArray
        Shape (num, label_num) or (num,)
    label : nd.NDArray
        Shape (num, label_num) or (num,)
    num_class : int
    average : str

    Returns
    -------
    f1 : float
    """
    if pred.dtype != np.float32:
        pred = pred.astype(np.float32)
        label = label.astype(np.float32)
    assert num_class > 1
    assert pred.ndim == label.ndim
    if num_class == 2 and average == "micro":
        tp = nd.sum((pred == 1) * (label == 1)).asscalar()
        fp = nd.sum((pred == 1) * (label == 0)).asscalar()
        fn = nd.sum((pred == 0) * (label == 1)).asscalar()
        precision = float(tp) / (tp + fp)
        recall = float(tp) / (tp + fn)
        f1 = 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)
    else:
        assert num_class is not None
        pred_onehot = nd.one_hot(indices=pred, depth=num_class)
        label_onehot = nd.one_hot(indices=label, depth=num_class)
        tp = pred_onehot * label_onehot
        fp = pred_onehot * (1 - label_onehot)
        fn = (1 - pred_onehot) * label_onehot
        if average == "micro":
            tp = nd.sum(tp).asscalar()
            fp = nd.sum(fp).asscalar()
            fn = nd.sum(fn).asscalar()
            precision = float(tp) / (tp + fp)
            recall = float(tp) / (tp + fn)
            f1 = 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)
        elif average == "macro":
            if tp.ndim == 3:
                tp = nd.sum(tp, axis=(0, 1))
                fp = nd.sum(fp, axis=(0, 1))
                fn = nd.sum(fn, axis=(0, 1))
            else:
                tp = nd.sum(tp, axis=0)
                fp = nd.sum(fp, axis=0)
                fn = nd.sum(fn, axis=0)
            precision = nd.mean(tp / (tp + fp)).asscalar()
            recall = nd.mean(tp / (tp + fn)).asscalar()
            f1 = 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)
        else:
            raise NotImplementedError
    return f1
```
May be useful if we want to accelerate the F1 score computation in the future. Also, we can take advantage of the fact that the [micro F1 is equivalent to accuracy for single-label classification](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37358496/is-f1-micro-the-same-as-accuracy) to accelerate the computatoin.

Core functionality for supported languages (namely Python, Scala, R, Julia, C++ and Perl) is delivered through bindings to MXNet's C API (see [c_api.h](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/include/mxnet/c_api.h) and [c_predict_api.h](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/include/mxnet/c_predict_api.h)). 

Each language's wrapper (such as [R-package](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/tree/master/R-package), [cpp-package](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/tree/master/cpp-package), etc) adds its own language-specific conveniences. For instance, the C++ package adds some OOP friendliness to the C functionality. 

Naturally, it does not make sense and in many cases would not be not possible to include those language-specific conveniences in the C API. So, admittedly, there are numerous aspects of Gluon that cannot be promoted to the core C API.

But, it seems like Gluon is going in a direction that will lead to a lot of great functionality that could and should be included in the C API. For instance, [rnn_cell.py](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/gluon/rnn/rnn_cell.py) and [rnn_layer.py](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/gluon/rnn/rnn_layer.py). RNNs are highly relevant to ML work being done in any of the supported languages. Sure, someone using one of those languages could implement equivalent functionality for themselves. But, I think such a concession diminishes the overall value of MXNet / Gluon.

As I said in my original post, TensorFlow has already made this mistake. Hopefully, they will back out of it. But why make the same one in MXNet? Python is clearly a preferred language for ML. I use it a lot and am a fan. But there are some important reasons for using MXNet in the other supported languages. Support within C and/or C++ means that native applications can take direct advantage of the functionality. The current thinking seems to be: "ML researchers develop models in Python, serialize them and then (maybe) serve them from C/C++" But that seems like somewhat short-term thinking. For MXNet's long-term flexibility and relevance, I think it is important to include as much as possible in the core C API.

Gotcha. I see and understand the problem now.

It feels to me like patching the `read` method isn't quite the right way to go. What we want is for the behavior of `get` to read defaults after `_cmd` configurations. What your patch does is remove all configuration variables that match a `_cmd` from the defaults. This creates the potential problem of using a `_cmd`, removing it, and then no longer having the default to fall back to.

Could you, instead, create a second parser for defaults, perhaps `self.defaults = ConfigParser()` inside `__init__`, load `DEFAULT_CONFIG` into there, and modify `read` to check `self.defaults` after `_cmd` but before failing?
I am OK with this for now but I think the scheduler should behave in a sane way when no dags are found.

This is caused by [``HttpFuture.result``](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado/blob/b6c381dbc771394d5ef7603ea1d369d78918ca62/bravado/http_future.py#L85).
Every time bravado receives an HTTP status code that is not 2xx it will raise a well defined exception (subclass of [``HTTPError``](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado/blob/v9.1.0/bravado/exception.py#L28)). The raised exception will have ``response`` attribute that will contain your response.
Note that disabling response validation does not prevent this behaviour, it just prevent to check the response body against the swagger specs.
Note that #38 allows a workaround to avoid this issue. We still need to catch the exception and handle it gracefully though.

> regarding inline require calls - is requirejs be able to pick up these pattern-option declared and only run-time known dependencies when building a bundle?
> I suspect that - although I wish it would, as it would give us a nice plugin mechanism.

As long as the requirejs define calls are loaded (via a compiled bundle or not) before the pattern at hand is invoked (which should be - as that latter code should only be started as body.ready is fired), passing the defined name into requirejs using `var mod = require(name)` will assign the module (or whatever defined) to `mod` in this example.

Can see an example of this in [this test](https://github.com/plone/mockup/blob/metatoaster-structure-master/mockup/tests/pattern-structure-test.js#L1576).  Note that it needs to be preloaded first asynchronously before it can be used as is within the code, which the existing framework can handle without issues.

```python
provideAdapter(DummySchemaExtender, name=u"dummy.extender")

# Clear cache
if CACHE_ENABLED:
       delattr(self.request, CACHE_KEY)

try:

        self.assertEqual("Document (1) - extended fields: 'dummy'",

finally:
       sm = getGlobalSiteManager()
       sm.unregisterAdapter(...)

```

```python
import datetime
import decimal
import platform

is_cpython = platform.python_implementation() == 'CPython'

def unix_time_millis(dt):
    td = dt - epoch
    # print('unix_time_millis td: {}'.format(td))
    ts = td.seconds
    tm = td.microseconds // 1000
    # print('td.seconds: {} td.milliseconds: {}, types: {} {}'.format(ts, tm, type(ts), type(tm)))
    rv = decimal.Decimal((ts * 1000) + tm)
    # print('unix_time_millis rv: {} type: {}'.format(rv, type(rv)))
    return rv


def datetime_from_unix_time_millis(ut):
    # print('datetime_from_unix_time_millis ut: {} type ut: {}'.format(ut, type(ut)))
    d = decimal.Decimal(ut) / 1000
    #print('ut / 1000: {} type: {}'.format(d, type(d)))
    if is_cpython:
        rv = datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(d)
    else:
        rv = datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(float(d))
    #print('datetime_from_unix_time_millis rv: {} type rv: {}'.format(rv, type(rv)))
    return rv

both changes fix the issue up to 6MB (not hitting TransientError: dbms.memory.heap.max_size). 
I never needed more than 4MB to start with, but now, while looking at your proposed fix and while testing both changes, I got a closer look at the io.py file trying to understand the ~6MB limit because both the 2.7 and 3.5 32bit interpreter seem to exhaust all addressable memory. 

After adding your changes, the problems became apparent:
1. recursive [ChunkedOutputBuffer.write](https://github.com/neo4j/neo4j-python-driver/blob/1.5/neo4j/bolt/io.py#L235) causes MemoryError
fixed with:
```python
    def write(self, b):
        new_data_start = 0
        new_data_size = len(b)
        while new_data_start < new_data_size:
            chunk_occupied = self._end - self._start
            chunk_remaining = self._max_chunk_size - chunk_occupied
            if chunk_remaining == 0:
                self.chunk()
                chunk_remaining = self._max_chunk_size
            chunk_write_size = min(chunk_remaining, new_data_size - new_data_start)
            new_end = self._end + chunk_write_size
            new_chunk_size = new_end - self._start
            self._data[self._end:new_end] = b[new_data_start:(new_data_start + chunk_write_size)]
            new_data_start += chunk_write_size
            self._end = new_end
            self._data[self._header:(self._header + 2)] = struct_pack(">H", new_chunk_size)
```
this seems to fix the problem for Python 2.7 as well as it drastically lowers the memory consumption but quite often the GC seems to have a hard time with the 8192 increment in [ChunkedInputBuffer.receive](https://github.com/neo4j/neo4j-python-driver/blob/1.5/neo4j/bolt/io.py#L142) which leads to excessive delays and/or hangs.
```python
        data_size = self._end - self._start
        if data_size > new_data_size:
            new_end = self._end + new_data_size
            self._data[self._end:new_end] = bytearray(data_size)
```
this dirty fix seems to eliminate the hangs but with larger values it may be impossible to have contiguous memory blocks.

Unfortunately, for Python 3.5 the next issue is raised:

2. recursive [MessageFrame.read](https://github.com/neo4j/neo4j-python-driver/blob/1.5/neo4j/bolt/io.py#L83) causes MemoryError

I believe the `select` syscall is related to the expect 100 continue processing we have.  We send the headers with the `expect` header set, and ensure we see a 100 continue response before sending the body.  As per the RFC we have a 1 second timeout where if we don't receive a response, we go ahead and just send the request anyways.  The debug logs will show if we do timeout waiting for the 100 continue response.
This is definitely something we'd like to have, but I can't comment on when it might be available.

Boto is just a thin wrapper around the service APIs exposing them as more idiomatic python. This would be a feature request for the EC2 service team and not Boto. We avoid implementing higher level logic on top of the service APIs because it causes feature disparity between the SDKs. If the service team implements the feature than all AWS SDKs will get the feature.

using the following helper function:
`
AWS_API_DEADLINE = 5                            # deadline for AWS calls (seconds)
AWS_SIGURL_EXP = 100                            # expiry of generate_presigned_url() URLs (seconds)

def aws_execute(client, method, par):
    # Perform an AWS method call by wrapping it in urlfetch.fetch()
    # Direct boto3.client.method() calls times out as GAE blocks it
    url = client.generate_presigned_url(method, Params=par, ExpiresIn=AWS_SIGURL_EXP, HttpMethod="GET")
    hdrs = {"Accept": "application/json", }                     # request JSON results in body.
    result = urlfetch.fetch(url, deadline=AWS_API_DEADLINE, headers=hdrs)
    if result.status_code // 100 != 2:                          # no 2xx from AWS?
        logging.info("AWS %s FAIL: %s", method, str(result))
        raise RuntimeError("AWS %s call failed with %d" % (method, result.status_code))
    if result.content:
        return json.loads(result.content)
    return {}
`

I think the way you are specifying the date value is off. Boto3 passes the literal value to the EC2 API. So right now it is passing `2018-05-11 19:37:01`, which is most likely a format that EC2 does not use. EC2 uses ISO8601 timestamp format (you can see this in their [API docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/APIReference/API_DescribeSpotInstanceRequests.html) as well) in their wire API, so I would recommend formatting the timestamp to be in that format. Let us know if that helps.

You're using the high level DynamoDB [Table](https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/dynamodb.html#table) abstraction which converts the raw API JSON to idiomatic Python types (in this case {"0": "string"} to a proper Python set). If you want to raw JSON responses from the API you should the [client API](https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/dynamodb.html#DynamoDB.Client.scan).

def get_member(name):

    mapping_table = {

    resource_class_dict = dict((k.lower(), ("{0}.{1}".format(v.__module__, k)))

    try:
        klass = locate(resource_class_dict[name.lower()])

I believe that [this code](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/88732b694068704cb151e0c4256a8e8d1adaff38/git/objects/submodule/base.py#L422-L424) is responsible for resolving to full paths.

The submodule system in GitPython was written to extend the implementation that git provided. Back in those days, git would not support the `branch` keyword at all, so it would just be ignored. Now it is picked up, and might not be what the git-submodule implementation expects.

Nevertheless, *origin/refs/heads/master* is usually a none-existing and incorrect branch path, and I don't know how that came to be.

I have no problem with lifting that filter. I put it in place mostly because I didn't think storing `__main__` can show anything meaningful (admittedly wrongly, as I think this is a valid use case in this issue). 

In recap, the problem I had (when I put in `-m` support and added that filter) is that my project has 3 different entry points - one backend server and two frontend cli utilities, and each entry point script, when collected, gets dropped into a single `__main__`, making it difficult to work with. Sometimes they'd even clobber each other.

Maybe yes another option would be collect it but maybe gate it from showing up by default in `list-modules`? Still gives people room to play with it progromatically. I don't see, for example, how `__main__` could work well with #26 otherwise.

> We could decide that it was a design mistake to not pass the whole frame to the code filter (which I think it was; there's really no downside, and it gives the code filter more info to work with). Problem is for back compat we'd have to introduce a new config method for "frame filters" and return support for code filters that take the code object.

I actually think this is a very good idea and might be worth the breaking change. I can't speak for others, but it wouldn't break anything for me since the default filter is good enough for my use cases.

@cdaringe @joshfriend, `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` was actually the default for the first few months of pipenv’s existence. We later moved to pew because there was demand from people using things like `VirtualenvWrapper` that wanted their environments to exist in a centralized place. It also prevents users from worrying about including their venv in their git repository. Each method has trade offs, and we’re never going to make everyone happy here. I’m also in your camp of preferring having the environment with the project, but realize we need a *STRONG* reason to break the existing functionality.

Currently, for most users it’s a binary decision. Either you want all of your environments with the project or you want them centrally managed. The flag allows people to opt into that and not have to change anything after which I think is reasonable. I’m happy to hear our more discussion, but realize that when this issue goes away because we’ve changed the default, another mirror will rise up with the opposite complaint and the cycle continues. Defaults are hard.

Just want to add a use-case of how the two groups are less-than-ideal, although workable:

I would suggest that "groups" are for humans. they are for helping us organize are minds and the projects our minds build. in rubygems land, groups can be arbitrarily named. assuming that prod and dev as the distinction is not something that ecosystem tries to impose.

For example, one project I'm interested in doesn't even use python on the production website, but has some processing tasks that happen locally to build assets. The two groups it wants to make distinction between are "devserver" (a mock api server that runs in python only locally) and "processing" (a bunch of long-compiling scientific packages for doing geospatial work). Forcing a project like this in the dev/prod dichotomy makes less sense.

Basically, I feel that the distinction is more opinionated than need be, and prevents to tool from speaking human in the way that users might like it to. In my example, we need to have a section to explain this, instead of just having intuitive names of the groups that can go in a command line flag :)

Anyhow, thanks so so much for all your hard work. Just wanted to provide a counter-point. Everything is work-aroundable, but I simply would contest that it shouldn't need to be -- it seems a reasonable ask. (fwiw, as someone who comes from php land, i feel rubygems and maybe go is the package management ecosystem to emulate, not composer)

@benoit-pierre Agreed, that's one of my biggest pet peeves with town crier.

For some background, town crier came out of Twisted, which has a [much more formal process](https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/wiki/ContributingToTwistedLabs) for contributions. For both Twisted and CPython, every PR must have an associated ticket in the bug tracker, and that's the number that's supposed to go in the changelog.

I think we were never quite so formal about it because we wanted people to be able to do drive-by PRs without an associated issue. If we want to alleviate the issue (which I agree is a pain), I see two ways forward:

1. All PRs must have an associated issue, and the news fragment is numbered by issue.
2. Change the workflow so that you can commit a file called `#PR_STUB#.change.rst` in the `changelog.d`, and we write a bot to update the PR when a number becomes available.

@jaraco We think adding this option will add more confusion since ez_setup already has a "to_dir" parameter, which sets where setuptools sould be downloaded as well.

In fact, if we change the behavior of `download_setuptools` in ez_setup, removing `if not os.path.exists(saveto)`, it will break bootstrap-buildout.py since --setuptools-to-dir starts "to_dir" parameter, *exactly* for reusing an already downloaded setuptools.

We're not confident into changing this code. It has the potential of breaking other's people infrastructure.

SInce the motivation of this issue was

> Shouldn't we have the same behavior in ez_setup.py? Always download but give the option to use a local copy, if needed? We're asking this because this "bad download" error is really confusing for beginners, since this is the official primary method of installation.

We started to think if we shouldn't just catch the `zipfile.BadZipfile: File is not a zip file` exception and add a message "Maybe you have a corrupt setuptools file. Remove it and run ez_setup again."

``` Python
import io
import pprint

import qrcode
import requests
import zbarlight

token = 'zbarlight'
versions = [8, 20, 32]  # range(1, 41)
box_sizes = range(1, 10)
border_sizes = range(0, 10)

failed = {
    'zbarlight': [],
    'all': [],
}
error_corrections = {
    'L': qrcode.constants.ERROR_CORRECT_L,
    'M': qrcode.constants.ERROR_CORRECT_M,
    'Q': qrcode.constants.ERROR_CORRECT_Q,
    'H': qrcode.constants.ERROR_CORRECT_H,
}

indent = '\t{}'.format(' ' * 8)
print('\t border:', *['{v:>{l}}'.format(v=i, l=len(error_corrections)) for i in border_sizes])
print('\t errors:', *[''.join(k for k in error_corrections) for _i in border_sizes])

for version in versions:
    print('\nversion={v:>02} ({m}x{m}): '.format(v=version, m=21 + (version - 1) * 4), end='')
    for box_size in box_sizes:
        print('\n\tbox={:>02}: '.format(box_size), end='')
        for border_size in border_sizes:
            print(end=' ')
            for correction_kind, error_correction_code in error_corrections.items():
                image_info = {
                    'version': version,
                    'box': box_size,
                    'border': border_size,
                    'correction': correction_kind
                }

                image = qrcode.make(
                    token,
                    version=version,
                    error_correction=error_correction_code,
                    box_size=box_size,
                    border=border_size,
                )
                tokens = zbarlight.scan_codes("qrcode", image)

                if tokens != [token.encode()]:
                    with io.BytesIO() as fp:
                        image.save(fp, format='png')
                        fp.seek(0)
                        response = requests.post(
                            'http://api.qrserver.com/v1/read-qr-code/',
                            files={'file': fp},
                        )
                    if response.json()[0]['symbol'][0]['data']:
                        failed['zbarlight'].append(image_info)
                        print('E', end='', flush=True)
                    else:
                        failed['all'].append(image_info)
                        print('!', end='', flush=True)
                else:
                    print('.', end='', flush=True)

print('\n\n{:=^100}'.format(' failed '))
pprint.pprint(failed)
```
The output of this script is below.
- a `.` means that zbarlight has decoded the QRcode
- a `! ` means that zbarlight and the online QR code reader have failed to decode the QRcode
- and an `E` means that only zbarlight failed to decode the QRcode

Oh, astropy_helpers.egg-info is always created--that's a normal part of package initialization.  The .egg-info directory for a Python distribution (now also called .dist-info, though setuptools still calls it .egg-info by default for historical reasons) contains all the metadata about the package.  It is used, for example, by the `pkg_resources` module to discover information about installed distributions.  In the case of astropy_helpers, it gets created in the ah_bootstrap.py script so that the astropy_helpers package can be found via the submodule.  

`git clean` from the root of your repo wouldn't normally remove it, since it's in a submodule.  But you shouldn't have to delete it either.  I don't think the .egg-info dir has anything to do with your problem.

So, I finally added the max-tree implementation. 

Max-trees are hierarchical image representations that can be used in many ways. Here, I implemented them for attribute operators. 

However, for the time being, I only implemented area openings / closings. I think that this would be enough for this PR, I can add other attribute operators later. 

The question now is: do we stick with the max-tree implementation or with the flooding implementation I made before? I think that the max-tree implementation is more elegant and more versatile (as it also extends to non-increasing criteria, such as circularity). My feeling is that we should keep just the max-tree implementation and remove the more traditional implementations. Any opinion? 

I also implemented `local_maxima`, which has exactly the same output as `extrema.local_maxima`. The function is slower (so not very interesting), but if I have already calculated the max-tree representation, it is much faster. So, I would keep this function for now, but maybe not expose it? 

So at the moment, this is not a huge add-on (finally only area openings/closings), but it is easy to extend.

The maintainer of the mingwpy toolchain, @carlk, described a diagnostic workaround in the topic https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/mingwpy/ndxqc80uqUA that I validated. According to his post, he plans to expose a configuration option in the next version of the mingwpy toolchain:

> To my best guess this error is due to the fact, that nupic may need long double with so called extended precision. long doubles in mingwpy have been mapped to double precision for MSVC and Windows API compatibility. This is not the case for the older so-called mingw-static archives on bitbucket.
>
> To verify this statement you can remove the occurences of: -mlong-double-64 in the sections: cc1_options and cc1plus in the specs file of the mingwpy compiler toolchain and test again with mingwpy. The next version of mingwpy should have the possibility to configure this behaviour properly during compile and link time.

Invenio v3 Framework is not released yet so documentation is not yet in a usable state. We do support custom storage backends. Essentially you need either a plugin for PyFilesystem that supports Google Cloud Storage or Amazon S3, or alternatively you need to implement a [storage class](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-files-rest/blob/master/invenio_files_rest/storage/base.py#L72) in Invenio that supports it (you'll need to implement open, delete, save and send_file methods).

Here's an example of the default [PyFilesystem storage class](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-files-rest/blob/master/invenio_files_rest/storage/pyfs.py), so you can see how much is involved in implementing it.

Here's an example of a [PyFilesystem plugin](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/xrootdpyfs/) for a protocol called XRootD.

Most likely the easiest is to just implement a storage class for Invenio.
