@jirikuncar happened to be in my office a few days ago and kindly reminded me that I should try to be more active in maintaining my PRs. I think raises is an important point: responsibility. We all have different positions here - some with low expectancies - but being an open source project we should at least try to find a personal point in what we do and motivate ourselves around it.

During a brief search I stumbled upon [ACM's Code of Ethics](http://www.acm.org/about/code-of-ethics) and I found paragraphs 1.3 and 2.6 to relate to my idea. The document is definitely broader in range, but I think it is a good read even if we don't end up integrating it.

---

Now, not only I think we should have this code of conduct, we should also hang it on our walls to remind ourselves how we should balance community, pleasure and work.

We should also not fear to reference this code and improve upon it. Hurting someone's feelings temporarily for the good of the entire community is a useful and valuable lesson for all.

makes sense, thanks!

> For reductions to be of significance to NE3 we should have the ability to perform them at any point (especially because NE3 supports multi-line operations) and thread them efficiently.

Agreed. I think threading is one of the performance wins for which I'm searching. Multiline exprs will probably come into play when I'm not switching back to native Python for my reduce operations. It's easy to conceptualise how having N cores each calculating the max of 1/N of an array, followed by a max of a len-N array would offer a speedup, but based on the below experiments, this requires finess to avoid Linux and Python implementation details.

Here're some synthetic benchmarks looking at different ways of taking the `max` of a 2**30 array of float64s - format is (output, duration of one run) as per the script below. I disabled dynamic cpu control and got fairly stable measurements.

```
('single threaded map-reduce', 0.99999999779209781, 0.39780282974243164)
('numexpr', array(0.9999999977920978), 2.0326719284057617)
('joblib insanity', 0.99999999779209781, 0.3058278560638428)
('numpy', 0.99999999779209781, 0.37674617767333984)
```

Joblib is pretty kludgy here, I was just validating that multicore map-reduce offers some measureable savings. Pretty sure it copies my array per child thread.


```
import numpy as np                                                                                                                                                                                                 
import joblib                                                                                                                                                                                                      
import time                                                                                                                                                                                                        
import numexpr                                                                                                                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
xs = np.random.random(2**30).astype('float64')                                                                                                                                                                     
spans = lambda n: np.linspace(0, xs.shape[0], num=n+1, dtype=int)                                                                                                                                                  
map_reduce = lambda n: np.max([np.max(xs[x:y]) for (x,y) in zip(spans(n),spans(n)[1:])])                                                                                                                           
divide_n_conquor = lambda n: np.max(joblib.Parallel(n_jobs=n, backend='threading') \                                                                                                                               
    (joblib.delayed(np.max)(xs[x:y]) for (x,y) in zip(spans(n),spans(n)[1:])) )                                                                                                                                    
divide_n_conquor2 = lambda n: np.max(n[0](joblib.delayed(np.max)(xs[x:y]) for (x,y) in zip(spans(n[1]),spans(n[1])[1:])) )                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
#warm up numexpr cache                                                                                                                                                                                             
numexpr.evaluate('max(xs)')                                                                                                                                                                                        
numexpr.evaluate('max(xs)')                                                                                                                                                                                        
print("starting benchmarks")                                                                                                                                                                                       
start = time.time()                                                                                                                                                                                                
print('single threaded map-reduce', map_reduce(4), time.time()-start)                                                                                                                                              
start = time.time()                                                                                                                                                                                                
print('numexpr', numexpr.evaluate('max(xs)'), time.time()-start)                                                                                                                                                   
start = time.time()                                                                                                                                                                                                
print('joblib insanity', divide_n_conquor(4), time.time()-start)                                                                                                                                                   
start = time.time()                                                                                                                                                                                                
print('numpy', np.max(xs), time.time()-start) 
```

@erikzenker Not a maintainer either, but I'm fairly familiar with the Brotli specification, albeit from a _decoder_ perspective rather than an encoder.

I am surprised that it should be possible to parallelize metablock encoding. It is possible that my understanding is incorrect. I will try to explain why I think that parallel encoding can't work with Brotli.

In Brotli, there are three mutable concepts that get initialized at the start of the stream, and then live across metablock boundaries. They have an impact on the decoding behavior of each block, and they also potantially get mutated within each block.

The three concepts are:
1. The output window (size defined by WSIZE in the stream header), initialized as empty. Used for back references, can span multiple blocks.
2. The literals buffer of the last two literals, referred to as p1 and p2 in the specification, initialized as p1 = 0, p2 = 0. Used to calculate Context ID for literals.
3. The distances (ring) buffer of the last four distances, initalized as [4, 11, 15, 16], where 4 is the most recent distance. Used in the decoding of distances, where distance codes can refer to these values.

As far as I understand it, parallel decoding of blocks is not possible because of those concepts, since subsequent bytes may depend on the state of any of these three concepts, which might have been mutated by actions on earlier compressed data.

Unfortunately, all of the test cases that actually contain multiple metablocks are structured in a way that doesn't test for this. I.e. in all of those cases, all of the output data is really encoded in just one of the metablocks. Because of that, the three stream-wide concepts above do not actually get mutated in those test cases, and thus your parallel implementation passes, when really, it should conceptually fail.

Hi @KhasanovBI ,
Thank you for your contributions.

I have some comments about your thoughts.

1 -  The name `SerializableRelatedField ` is too generic. In fact all of the `RelatedField`s are `serializable`. 

`PrimaryKeyRelatedField` takes `pk` as data, `SlugRelatedField` takes `slug` as data ... That's the way how they named. They use `pk` or `slug` to make a query and get the object.  `SerializableRelatedField `  takes `pk` as data. So it's in fact works like `PrimaryKeyRelatedField` but the way they represented is different. 

I would not name it as `SerializableRelatedField`. There is no way to understand it uses `pk` to retrieve the object.

2 - How is this different than other related fields? https://github.com/Hipo/drf-extra-fields/pull/23/files#diff-c00c8fa4b372409f5e72b508ae17f35dR190  Here it makes a query for each item in the list. Did i miss something?

3 - `Explicit is better than implicit`. I would definitely force developers to provide a queryset. But if you are getting the model from provided serializer and preparing the queryset, you should use default manager. https://github.com/Hipo/drf-extra-fields/pull/23/files#diff-c00c8fa4b372409f5e72b508ae17f35dR184  Here it uses `objects`. 

4 -  `PresentablePrimaryKeyRelatedField` extends the `PrimaryKeyRelatedField` and overrides `to_representation` method. So it only need tests for that method. Rest is handled by DRF itself. I trust Tom on this:P

The real bug is that swaggerhub autogenerated controllers use incorrect inputs when using underscores instead of camelCase.

When you use lowercase_with_underscores naming style the autogenerator changes it to camelCase while still expecting lowercase_with_underscores.

So this spec:
```
  /rebot:
    post:
      tags:
      - "rebot"
      summary: "rebot commands"
      description: "Runs a rebot command"
      operationId: "controllers.rebot_controller.rebot_post"
      consumes:
      - "application/json"
      produces:
      - "application/json"
      parameters:
      - in: "body"
        name: "rebot_input"
        required: false
        schema:
          $ref: "#/definitions/rebot_input"
      responses:
        200:
          description: "successful operation"
          schema:
            $ref: "#/definitions/cli_response"
        400:
          description: "invalid input"
      x-tags:
      - tag: "rebot"
```
With the important line being ` name: "rebot_input"`

Will generate this in rebot_controller:
```
def rebot_post(rebotInput = None) -> str:
    return 'do some magic!'
```

When it should be:
```
def rebot_post(rebot_input = None) -> str:
    return 'do some magic!'
```

More of a minor annoyance than a serious bug. I've reopened anyway for visibility. Feel free to close again if desired.

In your environment, does a socket timeout happen frequently when attempting to obtain a pyral Rally instance?  For the most part, the AgileCentral SaaS infrastructure will not have a problem responding within the INITIAL_REQUEST_TIME_LIMIT (currently set at 5 seconds).  The more common circumstance will be that other network factors such as proxies, router settings, etc. will sometimes either not be set up correctly or under some sort of load wholly independent of AgileCentral.  I'm reluctant to expose setting of the INITIAL_REQUEST_TIME_LIMIT as there is a workaround of catching the exception in your application's driver code and retrying the "connection".  Of course, you are always free to make a local modification and set the INITIAL_REQUEST_TIME_LIMIT to a value that works in your particular environment.

> the proper semver answer would be: don't add breaking changes unless it is really necessary

No, I think the proper semver answer is: if you add breaking changes, bump the major version number.

SemVer does not give any suggestion on a release strategy. It is just a convention that ensures interoperability.

## I would personally recommend (and personally follow) the following:

- At the first breaking change on the current branch, create and publish new prerelease branch.

> Example: current latest is `4.1.15`. I add breaking change B. I commit, tag and possibly publish `5.0.0-alpha.1` that contains B, and don't do anything on `v4`.

- I accumulate changes in the prerelease branch until some event (time elapsed since the last major version, importance of the accumulated changes, one specific urgent change…) makes me decide to release the new major version as the new official release. I then do an upstream merge of all the possible patches that were in the main release.

> Example: when I reach `5.0.0-alpha.6`, I realise it's been three months since `v4` was released, and I get a feature request for the third time that was added in `5.0.0-alpha.4`. I back port `4.1.19` into `5.0.0-alpha.6`, and release it as `5.0.0`.

## Optimisation suggestions

1. Release beta prereleases.

> In example 2, rather than publishing `5.0.0-alpha.6` to `5.0.0` directly, I would rather publish `5.0.0-beta.1` and wait a fixed amount of time until I'm sure enough clients have upgraded through the beta channel to catch bugs. If nothing happens, I just publish `5.0.0` as the exact same version as `5.0.0-beta.1`.

2. Publish a deprecating minor update when the new major is released.

> When `5.0.0` is released, publish `4.2.0` that just warns on all calls that include breaking changes in `5.0.0`.

@fpagnoux came to see me, we found the source of the slowness and I'm implementing a fix.

The reason is that the legislation cache (when using `legislation_at`) is stored in `TaxBenefitSystem` instances (`Reform` is a child class), and `Reform` instances are built at each HTTP request in the API.

We need to cache the `Reform` instances.

For the unit tests, the only instance which is kept between two tests is the base [`tax_benefit_system`](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/a3772aef8b5e58876205b6c0bf30b1fcb888c06b/openfisca_france/tests/base.py#L39). We need to cache reforms for the tests too.

That question is a bit vague  ;)

I think it's always good to have some kind of reference to a description of the representation within the representation itself, for introspection. Thus, if `apiVersion` was an actually useful reference to some documented state of the code, it would be great to keep it.
At the moment, though, I don't think proper versioning has been enforced in OpenFisca, and that piece of infirmation is thus misleading.

If versioning information was to be stored in the output data, then I don't think a single version number would fit. Considering OpenFisca's architecture, it would probably be an object with version identifiers for each of the parts (Core, Parsers, France, API), as a change in any of those can change the output.
At least that's the consideration that made me create the [submodule wrapper](https://github.com/sgmap/openfisca) we use to ensure known stable points are reproducible.

Then transform the coordinates with:

trpos = tr.map(pos)

and then renormalize the positions (see vispy issue above):

So here are my thoughts on this - based on the IRC chat and the patch.

I don't like the deprecation of 'str' descriptions in Python 2.7 - I don't see it buying us or our users anything other than strictness. We get that for free in 3.x, and a very large % of this patch is just scattering _u() around to accommodate this change. AFAICT its not required by having interfaces here. There is a minor thing, in that you've coded up deprecation logic, but the interface docstrings don't acknowledge this - if we do take this patch, I think its important to avoid user confusion by having these be internally consistent.

The z.i. adapter registry is in many ways an anti pattern that makes code harder to reason about, not better - glyph's blog post misses this in its praise for being able to pass in absolutely anything to a function using the global registry :).

AFAICT with the current patch, custom matchers doing their own mismatch and descriptions can be broken by this, because the patch no longer duck types. I don't think doing a major release is justified by the benefits of having better typing data - while I do want that typing data :). Update: you said on IRC that it falls back to x silently so isn't a break.

when mypy came out I did some experimentation with it, and got a bunch of bugs filed; they've mostly been fixed now, and I see from Guido's recent -ideas post that he's now keen on supporting Python 2.7 fully via comments (which is one of the things I raised, though I suspect thats lost in the mists of time :)) - so I think in short order we'll have mypy able to run sanely on testtools, if it doesn't already.

right now we already define the types - e.g. matchers.Mismatch - so we can allow folk to verify their custom matchers with mypy very very easily if we make sure that our code is annotated, or supply stubs ourselves - without requiring a second identical definition, or even abcs for most of our types. (Where things are abstract, abc is more relevant, but still perhaps overkill (and the global state thing again I don't like).

Thanks for the patch; its not actually a spelling mistake - https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/existant - though I certainly do grant that extistent is more common, and I'd be happy to change this to be easier for folk to read. However Jenkins seems to have lost its mind :(. Perhaps you could rebase and push again and see if that helps?

@tseaver you have a better feel for `datetime` than I do, WDYT about adding tz support to `_datetime_to_rfc3339`?

Some context for the overall number of methods, we essentially have to support all of the flavors of datetime from the various Google APIs:

- `_millis_from_datetime` (millisecond precision in BigQuery, `_millis` is a helper used only by `_millis_from_datetime`)
- `_datetime_from_microseconds` / `_microseconds_from_datetime` (the most common use case)
- `_rfc3339_to_datetime` / `_rfc3339_nanos_to_datetime` / `_datetime_to_rfc3339` (for APIs that use strings for datetimes, unfortunately some use microsecond precision and others use nanosecond precision, which Python's `datetime` doesn't support)
- `_pb_timestamp_to_datetime` / `_datetime_to_pb_timestamp` (for protobuf APIs)
- `_total_seconds_backport` / `_total_seconds` (legacy Py 2.6 support which will be sunsetted in #1607)

I can reproduce your errors for both, and neither are specific to `%pylab` in `IPython`.  It's a failure of the `*` import in `numpy` and `pylab`.  All of the objects just don't pickle as imported, so it's only an `IPython` concern in as much as they support a `pylab` mode.

I'm going to leave this ticket open as compatibility with `IPython` in `pylab` mode, and open a new ticket for the `numpy.int64` (and more generally `numpy import *`).  This ticket will continue for the `from pylab import *` part of the issue.

Did you take a look at this sample (cloudformation) snippet: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/quickref-autoscaling.html#scenario-as-notification ?

Troposphere uses the same properties as CloudFormation (but with python objects instead of json), so it should be pretty easy to convert.

BTW, some resource types might be trickier to document than others. I was just looking at issue #197 and I'm seeing discrepancies in the docs. Some examples:
- The Engine property isn't needed for a read replica but it doesn't state that as a condition.
- AllocatedStorage has "Required: Yes" but again is not needed for a read replica.
And there are quite a few constraints for what properties not to use when using SourceDBInstanceIdentifier.

Note: in some ways having a new resource type of AWS::RDS::DBInstanceReplica might make it easier to distinguish and specify the correct properties. In other words, then there could be less conditionals and more "required" fields.
Thank you for the PR and the additional rework.

First of all, thanks for the detailed response @scanny. 
I did not give the general implementation much thought, but I think it makes this project a bit more challenging. I've briefly looked into the XML reference, and you're right, there are a lot of different parent objects. 

## WordprocessingML  - documentation
Apparently, the bookmark XML predates the concepts of the XML:
`Within a WordprocessingML document, bookmarks refer to arbitrary regions of content which are bounded and
have a unique name associated with them.
Because bookmarks are a legacy word processing function which predates the concepts of XML and wellformedness,
they can start and end at any location within a document's contents and therefore must use the
"cross-structure" annotation format described in §2.13.2.
 `
This makes this certainly an even more interesting challenge, the only thing that is important is that a bookmarkStart element is joined with a bookmarkEnd element with an equal 'id'-attribute value. 

They present the following example, of a bookmark spanning a paragraph:
```XML
<w:p>
  <w:r>
    <w:t>Example</w:t>
  </w:r>
<w:bookmarkStart w:id="0" w:name="sampleBookmark" />
<w:r>
  <w:t xml:space="preserve"> text.</w:t>
  </w:r>
</w:p>
<w:p>
  <w:r>
    <w:t>Example</w:t>
  </w:r>
  <w:bookmarkEnd w:id="0" />
  <w:r>
    <w:t xml:space="preserve"> text.</w:t>
  </w:r>
</w:p>
```

It seems that therefore the element should be available at 'any' point. The document lists the possible parent objects as well. Its a large list at first, but a lot of it isn't implemented (yet) in the docx package. I've listed the possible parents, and grouped them a bit, sorted them based on the level and likelihood of implementation.


| Element       | Name          | Element Included?|
| :------------- |:-------------| ------------:|
| body     | body | yes |
|tbl | Table object | yes|
|tc| Table Cell | yes |
|tr |Table Row | yes|
|p|paragraph|yes|
|endnote| Endnote Content |no|
|footnote | footnote content|no|
|ftr|footer content|no| 
|hdr|header content|no|
|hyperlink|hyperlink |no|
| fldSimple| Simple field, determined by fieldcode|no|
|comment|comment|no|
| del |  deleted run content| no|
|ins| inserted run content (like tracked changes) |no|
|moveFrom|Move Source Run Content (i.e. tracked changes)|no|
|moveTo | Move Destination Run Content (i.e. tracked changes)|no|
|customXml|cell level custom xml|no|
|customXml|row level custom xml|no|
|customXml |inline-level custom xml | no| 
|customXml | block level custom xml |no|
|deg | math sign, degree| no|
|den | denominator (math) | no|
|e| specified base arg. of math func|no|
|lim|math, function name limit|no|
|num| math, numerator| no| 
|oMath|math, Office Math| no|
|fName | math, function apply obj. like sin, cos|no|
|sub| Subscript (Pre-Sub-Superscript)| **no?**|
|sup| Superscript (Superscript function)|**no?**|
|docPartBody | Contents of Glossary Document Entry|no|
|txbxContent |Rich Text Box Content Container|no|
|smartTag|specifies presence of a tag around inline structureslno|
|sdtContent|Block-Level Structured Document Tag Content|**no?**|
|sdtContent| Cell-Level Structured Document Tag Content | **no?**|
|sdtContent| Row-Level Structured Document Tag Content| **no?**|
|sdtContent |Inline-Level Structured Document Tag Content|**no?**|
|rt|Phonetic Guide Text|no| 
|rubyBase|Phonetic Guide Base Text|no| 

## Requirements:
For now just thought of two, probably more.
1. Must have an unique name
2. Must have a matching id-attribute.

## Custom Element Class
For the bookmark to work, we would need two custom element classes:
`CT_BookmarkStart` and `CT_BookmarkEnd`.

It appears LibreOffice (LO) is behaving rather badly in this case, perhaps as a compromise related to preferring ODF format.

It looks like LO takes all the table style value and applies them directly to each element of the table, including paragraphs in this case, where they overwrite whatever was applied directly. This has the additional implication that it effectively interrupts the style inheritance hierarchy for tables.

There's unfortunately nothing to be done on the python-docx side to remedy this. python-docx faithfully writes the `<w:jc w:val="right'/>` element on the paragraphs in question here, only to have that overwritten by LO Writer when it opens the file.

The only solution I can see is to avoid using a table style or to open the file in LO and let it have its way, and then adjust the file afterward to make the desired settings. I'd be inclined to prefer the former. I'm not sure LO wouldn't just overwrite custom settings again next time the file was opened if you took the latter approach.

You could avoid using a table style in cases where additional customization was required by applying all the formatting directly to the table elements after it was created; basically the same approach LO is taking.

The idea is that `CategoricalDomain` can be used to cast a feature into appropriate operational type (complementary to data type). For example, if you apply `ContinuousDomain` to some numeric column, then its valid value space will be encoded using an [`Interval` element] (http://dmg.org/pmml/v4-3/DataDictionary.html#xsdElement_Interval). However, if you apply `CategoricalDomain` to the same column, then its valid value space will be encoded using a list of [`Value` elements] (http://dmg.org/pmml/v4-3/DataDictionary.html#xsdElement_Value). This `Interval` vs. `Value` thing is singificant when validating new input data. By default, you will not be allowed to score the model with invalid data.

Hi, sorry, I forgot to comment earlier as I've been quite busy. I think it'd be good for us to chat so that we're all on the same page.

We already know that we can store WARC data in ipfs, the question is how best to do it. I think that we should try to standardize on an approach for ingesting WARCs and exporting them back out out of IPFS. After our last chat, we were waiting on the IPLD implementation, but perhaps now is the time to revisit this.

For some background, there is this comment thread ipfs/archives#28 as the main thread for web archive related activities.  There is also an implementation I created last year ikreymer/pywb-ipfs which provides a full browse, write WARC to IPFS, replay from IPFS solution. This was a demo with the full record being stored. This implementation provides a different way of storing works, separating the headers and the payload, which I think makes sense. I think the goal now is to iterate on this to make sure we know exactly how we want to proceed with storing WARCs.
One idea that was mentioned was even creating a standard `ipfs warc add` tool.

Once we have this figured out, I think we can then proceed to build more tools to use WARCs and IPFS. I already have many ideas, but am holding off until we have a standard approach. :)

I think more detailed and precised datetime information can be made available by adding raw request and response headers in the expanded banner. The FAB mode can still enjoy being minimal. Less precised human readable dates are still better than Date strings that are difficult for human to parse at a glance. If they do want precise time, the tooltip is there to help (that's how a very popular library moment.js does it too).

Once this CDXJ is in IPFS, we can use the sample data as benchmarks both for selective fetch and pywb's binary search once the selective data is fetched. There are a slew of GH tickets that could be spawned from this. ;)

Thanks for the additional information. That's actually not what the use_raw_enum_values header does; it doesn't modify the column headers. Instead, it will modify the content found in the rows to use enum values rather than display values.

The question is whether we should assume sorted.  My worry is that `amg_core` is expecting sorted  (for efficiency) but that we do not have any unit tests to expose this issue.  @jbschroder ?

In my test case for #204, the initial residue actually matches `norm(b - A*x0)` (not `norm(b)`) if I run the multigrid iteratively, not as a preconditioner. The scaling by `norm(b)` instead of `norm(r_0)` is due to the Rigal-Gâches theorem, so I wouldn't call it a "zero initial guess assumption" :wink: In any case, that's not how pyamg currently behaves.

The hypothetical `relative_residual=False` parameter would be equivalent to switching to absolute tolerance. I've seen somewhere a condition like `norm(r_k) < rel_tol * scaling_factor + abs_tol` with user input `rel_tol` and `abs_tol` being used, which might help with the problem you mentioned in https://github.com/pyamg/pyamg/issues/204#issuecomment-312694444. The big question, however, is whether `scaling_factor` should be `norm(r_0)` or `norm(b)`. I'd prefer the latter.

All this assumes that the tolerance factors are used only at the finest level. However, doesn't the current `tol` parameter affect also the accuracy of the coarser levels, smoothing or whatever? In that case using a different catch-all parameter(s) might be problematic...

Just wanted to point out another thing that we should consider when implementing the solution...

In the same scenario, if the child is closed, terminated, or killed by process B, it will become a zombie process (PID is still valid but the process is dead)...  This os.kill(pid, 0) solution would not catch that since the return would be None, instead of an exception.

The use case is like this:   1)  Process A created an child session 2) Process B is created by multiprocess and it uses the same child session 3)  If Process B kills the child session that was created under process A, os.kill(pid, 0) will return None and isalive will return True.

I'm not a huge fan of the models called "memory networks" - in general they are too tuned to a completely artificial task, and they don't work well on real data.  I implemented the "end-to-end memory network", for instance, and it has _three_ separate embedding layers (which is absolutely absurd if you want to apply it to real data).  @DeNeutoy implemented the DMN+.  It's not as egregious as the E2EMN, but still, I'd look at actual papers, not blogs, when deciding what methods actually work.  E.g., are there any memory networks on the [SQuAD leaderboard](https://rajpurkar.github.io/SQuAD-explorer/)?  On the [TriviaQA leaderboard](https://competitions.codalab.org/competitions/17208#results)?  On the leaderboard of any recent, popular dataset?  To be fair, more recent "memory networks" have modified their architectures so they're a lot more similar to things like the gated attention reader, which has actually performed well on real data.  But, it sure seems like no one is using them to accomplish state-of-the-art QA on real data these days.

Wow. The dense benchmark is now actually *faster* than the v0.3 code — 0.24 s/frame, down from 0.30. The sparse benchmark is still about twice as fast as v0.3, at 0.050 s/frame. Awesome! I think what @danielballan and I have learned previously is that when using the numba code, characterizing extra features costs almost nothing. So I think it is right to do only the final `drop_close()`. Note, however, that this introduces a new coupling between the peak-finding and refinement steps at a time when we are trying to decouple them. So maybe the default should be to perform KDTree filtering in `grey_dilation`, with an option to turn it off if you promise to do it later? There is still the disagreement in the results. In the figure, MATLAB is on the left and this new trackpy code is on the right. It looks like I can bring these back into agreement by changing the `percentile` parameter (another parameter for peak-finding). But I first want to make sure I understand what has changed. Am I right that 1. In v0.3, trackpy uses the feature *radius* for grey dilation, so that the overlap between feature ROIs can be as much as one radius; i.e., the minimum separation is one radius. KDTree filtering is applied *after* refinement, and controlled by the `separation` parameter, to set the final separation. 2. In this new code, `diameter` sets the size of the grey dilation kernel. There is still only one KDTree step, applied after refinement. 3. In the Blair-Dufresne code, square kernels are used everywhere, with size specified by a `separation`-like parameter that is independent of `diameter`. Effectively, there is no KDTree step. (I am fairly certain of this based on my reading of the code.) Is this more or less correct? Should `separation` set the size of the grey dilation kernel in trackpy? Conceptually, ROI size (for refinement) and neighborhood size (for peak finding and final separation) can be independent, as they are in the Blair-Dufresne code. Being able to set `separation` < `diameter` is useful when it's more important to find all the particles than to know their positions accurately.

It appears `uniform_filter1d` is our culprit. When it is applied to the image [here](https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/blob/5ec1e31093ba52645ff1e1dccdbaceeabcb783ef/trackpy/preprocessing.py#L77-L78), the output is different between scipy 0.19 and scipy 1.0.

To reproduce:

**Machine A: running Scipy 0.19**

```
>>> np.random.seed(1729)
>>> np.sum(uniform_filter1d(np.random.randint(0,255,(1200,1200)),19,0,mode='nearest',cval=0))
126475267
>>> np.mean(uniform_filter1d(np.random.randint(0,255,(1000,1000)),19,0,mode='nearest',cval=0))
126.50800099999999
```

**Machine B: running Scipy 1.0**

```
>>> np.random.seed(1729)
>>> np.sum(uniform_filter1d(np.random.randint(0,255,(1000,1000)),19,0,mode='nearest',cval=0))
126499353
>>> np.mean(uniform_filter1d(np.random.randint(0,255,(1000,1000)),19,0,mode='nearest',cval=0))
126.532032
```

The errors don't look like much, but they accumulate in subsequent steps.

In [scipy's `filters.py`](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/7a37db57132d18881d27812611b67158bc8b6d50/scipy/ndimage/filters.py) the filter is actually implemented in C - specifically, in [scipy's `ni_filters.c`](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/7a37db57132d18881d27812611b67158bc8b6d50/scipy/ndimage/src/ni_filters.c). Checking the history of that file reveals the culprit: roundoff errors in 0.19 that were fixed in [this commit (69977b0bcda8f121e66d3ee21c85424f9b291a92)](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/commit/69977b0bcda8f121e66d3ee21c85424f9b291a92#diff-6615c9e1da61874cfb06840164bd83e0).

I believe that both (1) and (2) were intentional. I routinely use `deisctl journal router` to quickly look at all the router journals.

How about this?

1. Look for an exact match (`deis-controller` matches only `deis-controller`)
2. If no exact match, look for a prefix match (`deis-router` matches `deis-router*`).
3. If no prefix match, prepend `deis-` and look for a prefix match (`controller` matches `deis-controller*`)
4. If no match, error.

@voith 
Honestly, I have not used the current version much. I know about the potential issues from doing the exact same approach in some of our older production code IE:
future = aio.run_coroutine_threadsafe(...)

The problems we saw, that are likely to apply here are:
1: The utility of an async await approach is limited by wrapping the coroutine such that the return value is future.result()
This prevents a true async system from being run. Granted I am performing the call in a different thread, but I still halt my main thread while waiting unless I re-wrap the original call up above. 

2: Although there seems to be timeout logic in the toolz, I do not see this in the actual provider class. This means I can halt on a loss of synchronization. 

3: If I want to gain back the ability to context switch the main thread while waiting on the response, I am now faced with a potential race condition. If I directly call the send method, without blocking while waiting on the result, there is no guarantee I will recv the correct response. IE If I am running a pubsub filter, or I am running multiple requests, another recv may eat the response. This may be fixed with locks, but this is not ideal. If this is the design paradigm web3py is committed to, may I suggest the use of this syntax instead:

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=XXX) as executor:

This in combination with the python websockets library, that also supports context managers for easy cleanup, creates a connection pooling system so that there is less pile up for the socket. If this method is used, there still must code that enforces the separation of pubsub based filters from polling filters and/or requests.

I can get around many of these problems by running multiple, independent, web3 instances up above and pooling the web3 instances into a/several asyncio.Queue objects ( because it allows blocking await syntax ). I can then pop the queue for a web3py class, or the right to use a class, and then push it back on the queue when done. The downside is this also feels very hacky due to the handling of exceptions before the object is pushed back to the Queue and the extra memory being used for what is functionally identical code.

Building general purpose async await frameworks is HARD. The tools that are in web3py are outstanding for many reasons. I think that providing a means for programmers to gain the benefit of the tooling while still being able to control the connections externally would be a great solution to generalizing the code without the headache of handling every possible implementation style. This allows developers the ability to choose to use the inbuilt connection system, that works for most applications just fine, or do fancy external stuff that just leverages the parsing and command creation system.

I just tested with this branch and tried to find the problem with the failing tests occurred in our sharding branch in py-evm.
In eth-tester, it seems we still need to handle the case when a transaction sent with raw_transaction(i.e. calls `send_raw_transaction`) is inside the pending block in manually mining mode.
In this case, since the transaction is already well-constructed and signed, there might not be the corresponding key in `PyEVMBackend`, therefore the `PyEVMBackend._key_lookup` will fail.

```
self = <eth_tester.backends.pyevm.main.PyEVMBackend object at 0x11007da90>
transaction = {'data': b'`\x005`\x1cRt\x01\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00` Ro\x7f\x...3', 'from': b'~s\x05\xb8\\\xf8\x03\xa4\xcf\x0c\x90_T\xcf\x1f\xf1\x92\xa8\xe6\xbe', 'gas': 3000000, 'gas_price': 1, ...}
block_number = 'latest'

    def _get_normalized_and_signed_evm_transaction(self, transaction, block_number='latest'):
>       signing_key = self._key_lookup[transaction['from']]
E       KeyError: b'~s\x05\xb8\\\xf8\x03\xa4\xcf\x0c\x90_T\xcf\x1f\xf1\x92\xa8\xe6\xbe'

~/eth_tester/backends/pyevm/main.py:397: KeyError
```

I'm still thinking how to solve it more elegantly.
My current thought options are:
1. Add a flag for each transaction in pending transaction pool, to indicate whether it is raw transaction or normal transaction dict. Besides, in `_add_transaction_to_pending_block` check if which type a transaction is, and respectively use `send_transaction` or `send_raw_transaction`
2. Change all transaction to raw_transaction(i.e. completely signed transaction in hex or `rlp.Serializable`). But it requires changes like adding API in `PyEVMBackend`.

The problem here is that `update --all` currently doesn't generally prioritize one package over another, even if that package is `conda`. So it decided to update `ruamel_yaml` even though it means `conda` has to be downgraded. The current version of `conda` has a restriction on which `ruamel_ymal` versions can be used so that it doesn't let you install the latest `conda` with the latest `ruamel_yaml` at the same time.
Newer versions of both, `conda` as well as `ruamel_yaml`, will be available soon which will resolve your issue.

Inspecting the code a bit, I assume that the issue is that the imported `conda_env` has been deleted and the `get_installer` call imports `conda_env.installers.pip` *after* the conda installer has removed the files (by switching from 3.5/site-packages to 3.6/site-packages). I realized that Travis CI runs on Ubuntu where the default shell is dash (yay). So I'm working to add dash support as well.

One problem with dash is that it doesn't support parameter passing to sourced scripts. My workaround is to have users set appropriate environment variables before sourcing:

```
CONDA_ENVNAME="envname" . activate
CONDA_HELP=true . activate
```

This issue should be fixed. The relevant fixed packages were:

python-2.7.10-4.
python-3.4.3-4
python-3.5.0-3

conda-env-2.4.4 (build 2)
menuinst-1.2.2

The fix is that the conda-env post-link script has been removed. It has been replaced by a modified Lib/site.py file in each Python installation, plus a library_bin.pth file in each Python installation. The combination of these lead to Library/bin being added to os.environ["PATH"] for any Python process. Any programs you install that end up in Library/bin (for example, patch) will still not be on PATH for older installations. You can either modify PATH to add Library/bin yourself, or uninstall and reinstall using a new installer (Anaconda >= 2.4.0; Miniconda >= 3.18)

Due to the nature of metapackages, ```conda install anaconda=2.4``` will actually NOT fix this problem.  The anaconda metapackage represents a frozen state of the distribution, which in this case exhibits an issue wherein DLLs located within the Library/bin folder are not found.  It would, however, work to say:

```
conda install anaconda=2.4
conda update --all
```

because the Library/bin PATH gets added by Python itself in the above mentioned packages, rather than depending on this being set in your system's PATH environment variable.

However, this then leads to the current issue on Python 3 that our package index is in a bad state.  Sorry, I'm new, and @ilanschnell, the master of this stuff, is on vacation until Wednesday.  I'm trying to rebuild packages to restore the index, but have not succeeded thus far.
Could this patching be done as part of the build step with `$PREFIX` expanded? I think then `conda-build` should pick this up. Or was this already tried and/or I'm just missing it?

Nano Server does not include MSI as an installation technology due to dependencies and the open ended nature of MSI custom actions. See full blog [here](https://cloudblogs.microsoft.com/windowsserver/2015/11/16/moving-to-nano-server-the-new-deployment-option-in-windows-server-2016/).

The only way I can suggest is you install the msi in a full window OS, back-up the `C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft SDKs\Azure\CLI2` and you can then xcopy to any nano servers. Do remember to add `C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft SDKs\Azure\CLI2\wbin` (or others if the destination folder changes) to the PATH environment though.

I don't believe we will support `WSA` for nano in any near future. So i am closing this.

```
Arguments
    --port   [Required]: The port or port range (ex: 80-100) to open inbound traffic to. Use '*' to
                         allow traffic to all ports.
    --priority         : Rule priority, between 100 (highest priority) and 4096 (lowest priority).
                         Must be unique for each rule in the collection.  Default: 900.
```

So you have a couple options:
1. Specify a range of ports and issue one command
2. Specify each port or port range with separate calls and specify the priority (you can omit on the first call if you are okay with the default of 900).

What version azure-cli and azure-batch-cli-extension do you use?
The latest version azure-cli should be 2.0.31, and azure-batch-cli-extension version should be 2.2.1. 
Please upgrade to latest cli and extension. 

However, there has a compatible issue between latest cli and batch-extensions, so you have to manually update dependency `azure-batch-extensions` to 1.1.1.  

I don't have MacOS on hand, but if you fresh install azure-cli in Ubuntu, you can run following command to update the component:
```
sudo /opt/az/bin/python3 -m pip install -U azure-batch-extensions==1.1.1 
```

If you run azure-cli on windows, the following command is used to update the component:
```
"C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft SDKs\Azure\CLI2\python.exe" -m pip install -U azure-batch-extensions==1.1.1 
```

The compatible issue should be fixed at next azure-cli release.

Are you using pybullet / Python bindings? If so, each object has a unique id, and the collision results will provide the body unique ids (and link indices). See http://pybullet.org
The underlying C++ implementation is here: https://github.com/erwincoumans/bullet3/blob/master/examples/SharedMemory/PhysicsServerCommandProcessor.cpp#L5305
There is a collision query for existing contact points (after step Simulation) and a query to compute closest points between arbitrary object(s). The object unique id can be stored in the btCollisionObject/btMultiBody/btRigidBody user info (for example getUserIndex2), or you can store the mapping between rigid body and unique id explicitly in some custom efficient data structure (hash map etc).

I had my doubts about the parsers and how to implement the signals triggered by sending or receiving some pieces of the HTTP protocol. The current implementation is just an intention or a POC of how other signals that have more granularity such as `on_content_chunk_sent` could be implemented, and I have my own rationale for that ( Obviously I can have it in a wrong way)

But I would prefer to get rid of this variable and try to put all of us on the same page. My proposal here is get rid of the `on_headers_sent`, `on_headers_received`, `on_content_sent`, `on_content_received` signals to be implemented later, and if we consider that they are a must implement them together with the `chunk` ones. It is gonna give us the freedom of focus our efforts right now in a set of signals that already give a user value.

What do you think  @asvetlov and @jettify ?

This was already discussed. This not going to happen, not in this way.

I see two possible solution:

1. Each chunk is separate stream.
2. Parser should emit end of chunk. It might be callback or special value.

Solution should not affect users who does not care about transfer encoding.
And sign question about context managers. It's strange that they doesn't helps.

I think it's a good idea to enforce PR authors to backport their code into stable branches.
Of course this should be documented and a note added to PR template.
I suggest whenever author identifies that code should be backported it's up to them to create a sibling PRs. Otherwise, maintainers should ask them to create additional PR.
I guess all PRs should be merged altogether when all of them are ready, not just main one.
But review could be done w/o having existing backport PRs.

1. I'm not clear why the root directory of the server should be secret.
  - So long as our other security measures are working, only the authenticated user can see this anyway; we're not broadcasting it to the internet.
  - We avoid *serving files* from outside the server root directory to make it marginally trickier for someone who can bypass those security mechanisms to read e.g. ssh keys. This has always been somewhat doubtful as a security mechanism, and I don't think that it's worth 'protecting' the information of which directory the server is serving like this.
  - It's no less protected than the list of files in that directory, which seems more likely to contain sensitive information than the path.
  - +1 to compressing the user's home directory to `~` as @Carreau suggested - does that make it more tolerable?
2. We already show the path from the server root directory in the move dialog. We're discussing this because we're concerned that it's not obvious to users how these paths work. I think showing the server root in the dialog too makes it significantly clearer; I don't think the absence of a leading slash makes much difference.

I agree with the rationale here, patch seems straightforward enough.

> @lkraider, @bintoro's concerns with removing `keys()` in favor of `(name, value)` iteration is that the user can break `dict(instance)` behavior by adding a `keys` field to their model.

Exactly. If we want to make `dict(instance)` reliable, we absolutely 100% must protect the `keys` attribute. There can be no user-defined attribute named `"keys"`, ever. Therefore, what's the point of not implementing the method?

Furthermore, there's no way we can sometimes have `iter(instance)` produce key–value pairs and sometimes keys. That's just too weird and too prone to breakage.

Now, I don't care about `dict(instance)` at all. I don't care about having `keys()` available, and I don't even care about `items()`. What I care about is being able to offer a full `MutableMapping` mixin so that it makes sense as an addition to the plain `Model` and that the two are consistent.

Let's keep in mind that no matter what is produced by `iter(instance)`, it's of limited utility, as is `items()`. Serializables are ignored, and submodels will be returned as intact instances. These methods are trivial because they are just raw dumps of `_data`. Who cares?

It would be much more fruitful to focus on improving `atoms()` and making it configurable. I want to be able to specify what my iterator outputs, and that's not gonna happen with `iter(instance)`.

> So how about the plan that I laid out from Feb 6th?

Well, given the above considerations, I guess I'm looking for some elaboration on what we would gain from changing the iteration behavior. The method footprint is the same in both alternatives, assuming we move `values()` and `items()` into a mixin.

This is a good insight but I'm afraid you've got it the other way around. I have been running your code in a debugger and I was checking how the x_beacon and y_beacon actually correspond to points on screen in the visualization. They are flipped, so x_beacon is the y coordinate and y_beacon is the x coordinate.

The reason for that is in the way the observations are presented. You have a 2D array in which every row corresponds to a row of pixels on the screen. This naturally brings about inverse index ordering. So the first index into the 2D observation array indexes the rows, and the row index on the screen correspond to the y-coordinate and the index within a row corresponds to the x-coordinate. So the ordering in the output is actually the way you'd expect it, it's just that the natural ordering on the observation array induces reverse indexing.

If they have done it in such a way that the x-index would be first and the y-index would be second, if you would simply print the array or look at it in a debugger, it would be transposed.

For clarity, try running the following script to see what I mean

```
import numpy as np

beacon_loc = np.zeros((8,8))

for i in range(5,8):
    for j in range(1,4):
        beacon_loc[i,j] = 1

print(beacon_loc)

x_beacon, y_beacon = [int(np.mean(c)) for c in np.where(beacon_loc)]

print('this is actually the y-coordinate {}'.format(x_beacon))
print('this is actually the x-coordinate {}'.format(y_beacon))
```

Just as a final note, this seems to be an issue when you are working with scripted agents but it didn't really apply for my case when using conv nets and a flattened spatial output (because flattening a 2D array and recomputing correctly the x,y coords from the 1D index gives you the right coordinates right away). So just think carefully about what you are using and what you need.

That has to do with Python's import order and only happens when importing from the source checkout directory.

There's a package called "postal" at the root of the repo. If you run ```python setup.py install``` from a source checkout, setup.py builds the C extensions (_expand.so and _parser.so) in a temporary build directory and then copies that into your site-packages. However, when trying to import one of the modules from that same checkout dir, Python will import the local package before checking site-packages, and the local package doesn't have the required C extensions. When running ```python setup.py develop``` or ```python setup.py build_ext --inplace```, the C extensions are built in-place.

The recommended way to install pypostal is either to use pip (it's on PyPI now so ```pip install postal``` does the trick, although the libpostal C library needs to be installed separately) or when running tests/developing locally, to use ```python setup.py develop``` as you mentioned.

Can't help with that RSEM error, never seen it before.

* namespaces do for images, instances and clusters what directories do for files on a file system
* their names always begin and end with a slash (unlike directory)
* they can be nested as in `/foo/bar/` but the nesting is largely inconsequential, IOW. `/foo/bar/` is just as isolated from `/foo/` as `/bar/` would be.
* the default namespace is `/__me__/` where
* `__me__` is a placeholder for A) your IAM user name minus the @ and anything thereafter or B) the value of the `CGCLOUD_ME` environment variable
* any IAM user can switch to any namespace at any time. Namespaces have no impact on permissions or access. They are merely an organizational tool
* the `CGCLOUD_NAMESPACE` variable sets the default namespace
* the `-n` command line option overrides the default for a single invocation of cgcloud

By using the / namespace you indicate that you are creating a cluster shared by the team. But again, this is just a convention. It would not be a problem if you did everything in `/jpdna/`, just slightly more confusing. Confusing for us, but mostly for you since you might also want to run an independent, personal experiment/cluster in that namespace.

Hey @betamos,

This is what #18 is about... unfortunately nobody got the time to work on it yet.

Here's a solution that works at my workplace:

1. Mark the tests you want to execute serially with a special mark, say `serial`:

    ```python
    @pytest.mark.serial
    class Test:        
        ...

    @pytest.mark.serial    
    def test_foo():
        ...
    ```


2. Execute your parallel tests, excluding those with the `serial` mark:

  ```
  $ py.test -n auto -m "not serial"
  ```

3. Next, execute your serial tests in a separate session:

  ```
  $ py.test -n0 -m "serial"
  ```

This works perfectly fine in C.I. servers, since it is usually trivial to add an additional command-line to the job.

I tried something like this. The problem is that because the code picks a quarter of tests for initial distribution and rounds down, it can end up distributing 1 test to every node, but with some tests left over in pending. Then `shutdown` is never sent and tests never run.

Here's another shot at simplifying:
```
        initial_batch = len(self.pending) // 4
        if initial_batch < 2 * len(self.nodes):
            # if we don't have at least two tests per node, we have to
            # send them all so that we can send shutdown signals and
            # get all nodes working
            initial_batch = len(self.pending)
            
        # distribute tests round-robin up to the batch size (or until we run out)
        nodes = itertools.cycle(self.nodes)
        for i in xrange(initial_batch):
            self._send_tests(nodes.next(), 1)
            
        if not self.pending:
            # initial distribution sent all tests, start node shutdown
            for node in self.nodes:
                node.shutdown()

Recording conversations for posterity:

RFC 2744 says:  

> Only valid if deleg_flag in ret_flags is true, in which case an explicit credential handle (i.e. not GSS_C_NO_CREDENTIAL) will be returned; if deleg_flag is false, gss_accept_context() will set this parameter to GSS_C_NO_CREDENTIAL.

RFC 2743 says (in Inquire_context):

> GSS_S_COMPLETE indicates that the referenced context is valid and that deleg_state, mutual_state, replay_det_state, sequence_state, anon_state, trans_state, prot_ready_state, conf_avail, integ_avail, locally_initiated, and open return values describe the corresponding characteristics of the context.

And says for init_sec_context (also applying to accept_sec_context):

> These state indicators' _[deleg_state, etc]_ values are undefined unless either the routine's major_status indicates GSS_S_COMPLETE, or TRUE prot_ready_state is returned along with GSS_S_CONTINUE_NEEDED major_status; for the latter case, it is possible that additional features, not confirmed or indicated along with TRUE prot_ready_state, will be confirmed and indicated when GSS_S_COMPLETE is subsequently returned.

So it looks like we should check for `COMPLETE or (prot_ready and CONTINUE)`, and then check `DELEG_FLAG` (the equivalent of `deleg_state` in 2744).

```

Of course it won't receive such amount ever so it halts there and never ends. Now, why that amount, which appears several times in the log, is something I don't know. You could also have tried different connection modes, or tagging the clients with some unique ID for each instance to know which fails (exported or main?), but I understand how tiring it is to debug this stuff and appreciate the effort so far.

If you read the `MTProtoSender`, you will see that only the receive and send loop ever receive or send things from and to the network, so a race condition seems unlikely. Maybe an `asyncio.Lock` around the entire send and receive could help as well, just to make sure if it's a race condition, or if we ever read the wrong amount.

I ran into a funky edge case that has security implications. I reported the underlying issue as #772.

```py
class ChildSchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.Field()
    bar = fields.Field()

class ParentSchema(Schema):
    baz = fields.Nested(ChildSchema, only=('bar',))

schema = ParentSchema(only=('baz.foo',))
```

The result of the intersections of these `only` options is an empty set, which we treat as disabling the only filter...

This change to `only` would make it possible to get unfiltered data without explicitly supplying any falsy values to `only`, so I think #772 needs to be fixed first.

The way we use Loom files is as (1) "a single branch". Our pipeline is based on [Luigi](https://github.com/spotify/luigi) from Spotify. You define a "task" which takes one or more input files and produces a single output. You string together tasks using these dependencies, and Luigi figures out what needs to be run, and what tasks can be run in parallel. (Luigi actually doesn't fit well with Loom because it tends to always want to make a new file for every step. This is something we'd like to fix.)

I would probably vote for going with one file, one branch, but maybe with concessions for multiple analyses in specific cases where it makes sense.
Thanks, Sten!

An easy change is to use `int16` (two bytes) instead of `float32` (4 bytes) to store the data. It will only reduce the file size slightly, though, because float32 compresses much better (since the two high-order bytes are mostly zeros). 

Another possibility is to sort the rows (genes) by expression level, so that the bottom half or so of the matrix is all zeros. That's probably a major win, but you'd first have to create the file. Something like this (not tested):

```
with loompy.connect("1M_cells.loom") as ds:
    nnz = ds.map([np.count_nonzero], axis=1)[0]
    ordering = np.argsort(-nnz)
    ds.permute(ordering, axis=0)
```

After this, you'd probably have to copy the data over to a new file (i.e. using `ds.scan()`) to force HDF5 to reclaim the space gained by the improved compression.

We use a chunk size of (64, 64) which was optimized for speed, not compression. It's possible that larger chunk sizes could be more efficiently compressed, but at the expense of slower access (especially random accesss of individual rows or columns, which needs to load entire chunks along one axis). There are also other compression methods than gzip, but only gzip is supported widely so you'd risk creating files that could not be read on all platforms. 

On the upside, 1.3 million cells in 9 GB is not too shabby, considering that even laptops have terabytes of disk these days! You'll be able to fit every single-cell experiment ever published on a 64 USB stick.

Hi @asyncee ,

First thing you can do is to pass `db` factory, but not a result of its work (`DB` instance) to `UserRepo`:

```python
db = providers.Factory(DB, db_url=Core.config.db.url)
user_repo = providers.Factory(UserRepo, db_factory=db.provider)

# and then:

class UserRepo:
    def __init__(self, db_factory):
        self.session = db_factory().get_session()
        assert isinstance(db_factory, providers.Factory)
```

One more option that I see is to create a custom builder for your repos:

```python

def create_repo(cls, db):
    return cls(db.get_session())

db = providers.Factory(DB, db_url=Core.config.db.url)
user_repo = providers.Factory(create_repo, cls=UserRepo, db=db)
```

Also previous example could evolve to [Factory Method](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Factory_method_pattern) pattern:

```python
class Repo:
    @classmethod
    def create(cls, db):
        return cls(db.get_session())

class UserRepo(Repo):
    def __init__(self, session):
        self.session = session


#  and then:

db = providers.Factory(DB, db_url=Core.config.db.url)
user_repo = providers.Factory(UserRepo.create, db=db)
```

PS: Just my subjective opinion for this particular case - I would prefer to use 3rd option.

Hope it helps,
Thanks,
Roman

All these examples look fine to me. 

For `(assert (not (distinct a a))) ` note that "distinct" means `(not (= ...))` and not `(not (fp.eq ...))`. So, `(not (distinct a a))` means `(= a a)`, which is indeed satisfiable, even if we substitute the (unique) SMT NaN. 

For all other examples, note the location of all `(pop)` commands: they pop the constraint that was just added, so all `(check-sat)` commands check an empty set of assertions, which is indeed satisfiable. If we move the pops one line down, the results are respecively unsat, sat, unsat, sat.

If there is good justification, I can add optional argument for this. I tend to think that optional fields are an all around lose-lose situation. They make development harder--everything that works with ROIs now has to always check for the existence of a unique ID, and it is fundamentally at odds with a standard--how can this be a standard if no field is ever guaranteed to exist. 

Personally, I think requiring a unique ID is perfectly reasonable; however, there has been a lot of pushback to make this component easier to use. I am happy to add unique IDs back in as a required field. If an optional field is really wanted, I will need some justification beyond mere usability to prioritize this feature.

> `Error: Module version mismatch. Expected 48, got 53.`

This error is due to the fact that `electron-builder` rebuilds the native module against Electron headers (`53`) to be able to use it. But the unit test run on your local Node.js version (`48`). 

Probably the easiest option is to avoid using `git-utils` in the tests and mock the behavior instead. Otherwise we would need to rebuild the module against the correct version.

Since this adds a new native dependency we would need to revert #1268 (except for the `package.json` changes). Or we ask the `git-utils` team if they would be interested in providing prebuilt binaries via [`prebuild`](https://github.com/mafintosh/prebuild) .

In this case, "the actual block" simply does not exist in memory - in some sense, it is a "virtual block". Semantics wise, `block.instructions` is equivalent to asking "what are the instructions that this virtual block contains?" And the answer is, "no, the virtual block _cannot_ contain any instruction since it's a virtual block, and here is an error for you so that you don't expect a virtual block to contain any instruction in the future."

> I think the proper solution is to return an empty set if the actual block doesn't contain any instructions.

Testing downloads is out of the scope of unit testing, as it necessarily has side effects (network communication is a side effect), relies on external resources, and makes many assumptions about the environment (internet connection that allows access to youtube, availability of a video (both that it hasn't been deleted yet and that it's available in the country being tested in), availability of soft dependencies, ability to write to file system, etc). It's the sort of thing that cannot be reliably tested by simple code, instead needing either a comprehensive integration test or (much simpler and more appropriate in this case) manual testing. This is *not* the sort of thing that can be tested as a unit, as it depends on too many external factors.

1. This getter is part of a descriptor (in fact our type `Descriptor`) and so is only called when doing `obj.name` which will call `type(obj).name.__get__(obj, type(obj))` (or equivalent). The only time a `Descriptor` is created is in `GangaMetaclass.__init__` and it only creates them for schema items.

The alternative is that `j = Job(); j.name` would not work (since there would, at that point, be no `'name'` entry in `_data`). The following seems to happen: 

1. user authenticates to Auth0 with Google identity
1. user obtains an Access Token that has openid scope, and thus allows to fetch user info
1. user sends access token to obtain user id from root URL
1. kinto iterates the configured policies to validate the access token, and tries to fetch the user info for that
1. since access token obtained from Auth0 allows to fetch profile from Google, kinto identifies the user with the google policy
1. kinto admin sees that user tried to identify with auth0 but obtains a user id prefixed with google and considers that something is wrong

Since Auth0 is a kind of bridge to Google (and other identity providers), I don't think it makes sense to have both configured, and we could consider it a limitation of the server.

> for some requests, it's slower than what we have

I don't think that's a fair evaluation. When there were only 10 records to be matched, the sum of the SELECT + COUNT query was 10% slower. Also, it was a difference between 1.48ms vs. 0.15ms+1.55ms. That might just be noise. There is admittedly a tiny overhead of having to send two distinct queries on the open connection. 

The proposed solution *works* for tiny queries. Paying 1.7ms for a count and a set of records is cheap. And it scales *rapidly* when it goes way beyond 10 matching records. 

Also, in terms of priorities I think we're missing a subtle point in that if a READ query takes 15 seconds (not unrealistic at all at the moment on databases like Buildhub) that means the Postgres server is potentially distracted and resource hogging for other queries (read or write) that needs to be dealt with. Perhaps that's why we saw, in New Relic, these monster times for queries that, on paper, should be fast.

The code in pytraj is very simple (and pytraj applied the same copy and paste strategy for other actions)

```python
@_register_pmap
@_super_dispatch(has_ref=True)
def calc_distance_rmsd(traj=None, ref=0, mask='', top=None, dtype='ndarray',
        frame_indices=None):
    '''compute distance rmsd between traj and reference

    Parameters
    ----------
    traj : Trajectory-like or iterator that produces Frame
    ref : {int, Frame}, default 0 (1st Frame) 
    mask : str
    top : Topology or str, optional, default None
    dtype : return dtype, default 'ndarray'

    Returns
    -------
    1D ndarray if dtype is 'ndarray' (default)

    Examples
    --------
    >>> import pytraj as pt
    >>> # compute distance_rmsd to last frame
    >>> pt.distance_rmsd(traj, ref=-1)

    >>> # compute distance_rmsd to first frame with mask = '@CA'
    >>> pt.distance_rmsd(traj, ref=0, mask='@CA')
    '''
    dslist = CpptrajDatasetList()
    command = mask
    _ref = _get_reference_from_traj(traj, ref)
    _top = _get_topology(traj, top)
    fi = _get_fiterator(traj, frame_indices)

    act = CpptrajActions.Action_DistRmsd()
    act(command, [_ref, fi], top=_top, dslist=dslist)

    # exclude ref value
    for d in dslist:
        d.data = d.data[1:]
    return _get_data_from_dtype(dslist, dtype=dtype)
```

@eLRuLL I recommend using bumpversion to do the releases.

You can create a `.bumpversion.cfg` config file with this:

```
[bumpversion]
current_version = 1.0.2
commit = True
tag = True
tag_name = v{new_version}

[bumpversion:file:setup.py]

[bumpversion:file:scrapy-crawlera/__init__.py]
```

and then you can use the bumpversion utility to create the releases (update version number and tag the commits).

@DanCardin @graingert @zzzeek 
If you are still having this need (or if some people have it in the future) of auto-generating the alembic migration script for some Redshift tables with columns encoding. 

Indeed it is currently not possible using Alembic but there is a workaround : write the migration script manually as following (using the example given by DanCardin)
```
def upgrade():
    from tables.models.example import Example
    Example.__table__.create(bind=op.get_bind())
```

Using this trick you avoid Alembic's code generation and use directly the sqlalchemy-redshift adaptor which itself is aware of the `info={'encode': 'lzo'}` field and will include it while creating the table.

As for passing outputs of a model.step into environment, the following snippet should do it (observation shape is specific to Reacher-v2):
```
import gym 
    import numpy as np
    env = gym.make('Reacher-v2')
    obs = np.zeros((1,11))
    obs[:] = env.reset()
    while True:
        actions, _, _, _ = model.step(obs)
        obs[:], _, _, _ = env.step(actions)
        env.render()
```

I actually just debugged this exact error on an Ubuntu 14.04 server. In my case (and hopefully yours), the issue was that Python was using NVIDIA's libgl, rather than Ubuntu's. My quickfix was to set: `LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/mesa/` — you may need to use a different path though.

Maybe it is not an issue with conan-package-tools. I recently changed the behavior of ``tools.vcvars_command()`` because it was a bit weird and problematic. So the current implementation is:

- It checks if the vcvars are already set, if they don't match the current conan settings, it will raise an error (this was silently ommitted before).
- If the vcvars are already set, and they match the current conan settings, return an empty string, to avoid calling it twice
- If the vcvars are not set, it returns an string with the command to set them.

So, conanfiles that want to concat the command must basically do:

```python
vcvars = tools.vcvars_command()
vcvars = vcvars + " && " if vcvars else ""
```

Sorry if it broke your build, I am trying to imagine some behavior that would be backwards compatible, but I am not aware of it. Any suggestion or ideas?

in cfn_api.py the wait_for_stack_action_to_complete() method is called on stack updates. This calls wait_for_stack_events() to wait for a UPDATE_IN_PROGRESS event and once again to wait for an UPDATE_COMPLETE event. The wait_for_stack_events() method raises Exceptions if f.e. a rollback occurs. This usually should never happen during it is called for the first time to wait for the UPDATE_IN_PROGRESS event. If one starts a sync process some seconds after a rollback occurred, the 10 seconds time jitter window leads to an exception being raised while waiting on the stack starting to update since the event from last update action occurs in stack events and falls within the interesting events time window. To fix this, I would need to do a lot of nasty stuff. Since this only occurs in very rare conditions, I wouldn't fix it now.

How does the current code handle when MAC is stopped in middle of an operation (scan/transmit data)?
and what should be done? 

Current model, if we are in middle of tx of frame, MAC will continue to do retx of the frame when MAC is stopped. 

I think when MAC is stopped, the current operation should gracefully stop and if there are any callbacks, they should be invoked with an error indicating that MAC was stopped. 

For a new opeation, we can either return a failure when the operation is scheduled from `SendFrameRequest()` or `ActiveScan(), `EnergyScan()`, etc.. or accept the new command/operation and return failure as part of its callback.

cc: @dbarrosop @mirceaulinic 

These are some notes for Python3 support from my investigation as part of the NAPALM hackathon.

The general process is:
1. Get napalm-base to support python3.
2. Cleanup napalm-base from a pylama perspective. This will make it easier to see issues introduced because of python3 conversion.
3. Thoroughly regression test existing drivers (both getters and config operations) to make sure conversion didn't break anything.
4. At that point, any individual NAPALM packaged could be upgraded to support python3.

Dependencies for napalm-base conversion.
1. pyangbind is not python3 compatible. This is by far the biggest issue that needs resolved (can we decouple openconfig support from napalm_base and/or not use pyangbind).
2. Convert over to using jtextfsm for textfsm support. Need to regression test that this works for both python2 and python3.
3. Other standard python2 / python3 converstion items:
    a. __future__ print_function and unicode_literals
    b. Minor item with exception
    c. Minor item with iteritems
    d. Imports within package need to be Python3 compliant.
    e. Cleanup expected test data types (unicode/str) so supports both Python2 and Python3.
    f. Eliminate any explicit unicode type conversions.

See:
https://github.com/ktbyers/napalm-base/tree/python3

I was able to reproduce the segfault on Python 2.7. The issue is with old-style classes which don't inherit from `object`. In `test_multiple_inheritance_mix1`, if I replace `class Base1` with `class Base1(object)`, everything is fine again.

The segfault itself happens in `type_caster_generic::load()` when iterating over the `tp_bases` tuple. It seems that with old-style Python classes it's possible that `tp_bases` remains uninitialized, randomly pointing to invalid memory. However, `tp_base` seems to be correctly set to `nullptr`. This is pretty strange and I wasn't able to find any mention of it in Python's docs, but replacing the `if (tobj->tp_bases)` check with a stronger `tobj->tp_base && tobj->tp_bases` seems to resolve the issue.

~~pkg_resources has a class called `WorkingSet` which holds the `iter_entry_points()` method, among others. It is implicitly instantiated to provide the global entrypoint-resolving routines we're all familiar with. However, I suspect we could instantiate our own, pointing at a different venv (or even non-venv, if we could find a nice way of installing things into it), and let people drop plugins into that. The rest of the machinery in pkg_resources looks like it handles all the variants of module-loading you could ever want. Will this work at all? We should play with a proof of concept.~~

We could do this easily by just adding a new folder to sys.path, either in some conventional place or in one pointed to by a piece of configuration.

Here's the tricky part: whatever plugins we install in the venv will bring along dependencies. If we don't add the venv to sys.path, the dependencies won't be found. If we prepend it, they'll shadow the ones in whatever env or venv certbot is running from. That might be acceptable, except that one of those dependencies is bound to be certbot itself, and we certainly don't want an un-hash-checked version of certbot coming in and taking precedence. However, certbot itself will have already been loaded in sys.modules by the time the venv is in play, so it itself won't be shadowed, but there is the potential for any of certbot's dependencies that haven't been imported yet to be shadowed. I wouldn't want to debug that nondeterminism. Thus, I think we'd have to put the new venv on sys.path *after* certbot's env. Consequently, third-party plugins will not effectively be able to require versions of libs newer than those in certbot itself. That seems an acceptable tradeoff to me, and of course we should make it explicit in the docs. This also softens the blow of un-hash-checkedness: only the plugin and any new packages it depends on would be unchecked. (That's still pretty wide-open, of course, but I think all our security guarantees are pretty much moot once you start plugging in third-party code as root.)

There's [some precedent](https://github.com/jsvine/envplus) for this sort of thing, which suggests there are no serious gotchas.

And the same technique should work, regardless of whether certbot-auto is in play.

```shell
pre-hook = /bin/run-parts /etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks/pre/
post-hook = /bin/run-parts /etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks/post/
renew-hook = /bin/run-parts /etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks/deploy/

or single commands (scripts)

pre-hook = systemctl stop nginx
