Thanks for the report @wowcut, we're always looking to improve our documentation!

> So what exactly is config-dir? Is it different to the directory that contains cli.ini?

It stores your cert data, as well as configuration information for how Certbot will function. We search the `renewal` subdirectory in `config-dir` for `cli.ini`. If it is not found, we search those other locations for `cli.ini` as well. So, here some things we could specify on [this page](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#configuration-file):
  - We search `config-dir` for `cli.ini` (in particular, if `config-dir` is set to something other than `/etc/letsencrypt`)
  - Which locations take priority over others.

Is there anything else that was unclear or underspecified here?

> And what happens if config-dir is set to a different place than work-dir?

They should be-- this is what happens [by default](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/a2222d5bdff304f8c55b63986d09d3f155ef6893/docs/cli-help.txt#L247).

> It would be great to read about these important details, as it may help to better adopt certbot to special requirements with local installation, e.g. in a multi-user operating system.

Ideally, the function of these directories should be opaque to the user unless they wish to provide a `cli.ini` configuration file. [This FAQ entry](https://certbot.eff.org/faq/#does-certbot-require-root-administrator-privileges) about running Certbot without elevated permissions may be of use to you if you haven't already seen it; we would appreciate suggestions on the documentation there as well!

> This would require some changes outside of our acme module, but what if when we updated an account from v1 to v2, we removed the symlink and wrote the ACMEv2 account to the ACMEv2 accounts directory and left the ACMEv1 account unmodified? This may require some changes to what we symlink if we want to try and keep our limited support for multiple accounts.
>
> Bonus points if we check for this case when deleting the account and deleted both versions if they exist. I think this could probably come later though.

An ACMEv2 account in the ACMEv1 directory with an ACMEv2 URL is not ideal. Trying to delete an account then later finding out it wasn't deleted is worse.

The reason it's not ideal is if values get put in there that break ACMEv1 code. But the reason we have them symlinked is that on the LE end they're actually the same account on the backend, it's just the middle layer that's talking that's different.

So assuming that the goal of account reuse is to not break accounts and keep them consistent, I actually think it's better to just update the URL, do unregistration properly using ACMEv2, and just save that back to the shared local storage. As long as that doesn't then break things when you try to use ACMEv1 (which I'm about to check).

So there are two main ways to get the code for certbot.

Double-encoding a query is a well known issue with a resolution to use NE. There are cases where NE isn't the answer. However, for a blind wildcard redirect to move a client from http -> https it is important to not alter any part of the URL beyond the protocol (i.e. path, port, query, etc.).

Failure example:
`http://foo.bar.com/apple?email=me%40domain.com`
incorrectly redirecting to:
`https://foo.bar.com/apple?email=me%2540domain.com`

There is no reliable manner for the receiving code to know if the received query string is single url encoded or erroneously double url encoded. Therefore, there is no legit legacy workaround coded by current certbot users. Anything written by someone out there...isn't reliable.

Therefore, the resolution is to fix the core issue which is the use of QSA. It should instead be NE. This will result in...

Good example:
`http://foo.bar.com/apple?email=me%40domain.com`
correctly redirecting to:
`https://foo.bar.com/apple?email=me%40domain.com`

I want to note that the way I handle single channel images with precision > 8-bit breaks from tradition to scale tensors to [0, 1] and images to [0, 255].
In my opinion this is a necessary change because we would loose precision and / or would need to specify types on every transform call. Nevertheless I would suggest to introduce a breaking api change with the next major release which stops scaling images to [0, 255] and tensors to [0, 1]. This is something the user can easily implement using `transforms.Lambda` or `transforms.Normalize`.

Hi Brian, great to hear from you!

There's definitely lots to work on. Regarding http: the main immediate prerequisite inside trio itself is TLS support (#9), which is very doable, though we need to sort out what our idea of a "stream" looks like (part of #73).

We also have some todo items to split out our pytest and sphinx infrastructure into reusable packages (#27, #71), which should be relatively straightforward and is something that any downstream projects are going to want.

Regarding aiohttp vs. h11/h2: I may be biased, but I would definitely try to build something on top of h11 rather than trying to do a direct port of aiohttp. My impression is that aiohttp's internals are... unnecessarily complex, and would need significant reworking to match the structure that trio requires. There definitely is a fair amount of stuff that a full fledged http client needs (I assume you're thinking mostly about clients?) that isn't in h11, though.

Another possibility is to look at urllib3; @Lukasa is working on porting it to use h11: https://github.com/shazow/urllib3/pull/1067
In the long run the ideal situation would probably be for urllib3 itself to gain the ability to run in "trio mode", because there is a ton of complicated stuff that a really high-quality http client has to do and there's less wasted work across the ecosystem if we can all share one implementation of most of it. It's possible that this will take long enough to become viable that we want something else as a stop-gap in the mean time; that could be a minimalist library written from scratch, or a hacky fork of @Lukasa's branch with a bunch of extra `await`s sprinkled about... I dunno. Just throwing it out there as something to think about.

Regarding porting synchronous libraries: I'm not sure I understand the question, I'm not familiar with the Elasticsearch library, and I've barely written any more trio code than you have ;-), so I'm not sure what to say. As a general rule of thumb, I think idiomatic trio APIs are going to look at lot like synchronous APIs, except with blocking operations becoming async and with most of the `timeout=` arguments stripped out.

Regarding the python-trio organization: that is one of the reasons I registered an organization, yes :-). We probably shouldn't overdo it with trying to drag every trio-using project in, but certainly for more central/official pieces it's a good place. (pytest-trio and sphinx-trio were the ones I immediately had in mind.)

@ncoghlan: it's off-topic here, but I would suggest that Python should also issue a warning on a bare `f.name` statement, which is also probably a mistake in most cases. The REPL isn't an obstacle; the parser knows whether a bare statement is going to be passed to `sys.displayhook` (cf `compile(... mode="single")`) and of course wouldn't issue a warning on such cases b/c they obviously aren't no-ops. The try-and-catch-`AttributeError`-or-`NameError` case is legitimate (it's the only case I could think of too), but there are other ways to handle that use case that are even shorter and just as clear (e.g. using `if hasattr(...)`), and it'd even be possible to write an automated fixup script to convert most cases (cf golang's nice use of this strategy). If the benefit is a smoother onramp for users in general then it seems plausibly worth it to me.

I think having a machine-readable version of the operator specification is good. The one suggestion I have is that it would be nice to have such a specification in one place. Currently, parts of the specification exist in the OperatorSetProto file. If the same could be expanded to include more metadata about operators, that would be advantageous. (E.g., see issue [https://github.com/onnx/onnx/issues/188])

So, here is the current proposal that I have been advocating for.

**Goals.**

* **Operator definitions will evolve.** We are not perfect, and sometimes we will put an operator in ONNX that is bad / doesn't stand the test of time. It must be possible to evolve operators, and clearly communicate what  the preferred form of an operator is.
* **Don't break models.** If a user exports a model with an old version of an exporter, as much as possible, that model should continue to import with newer versions of importers (or, at the very least, fail with a message stating that the version is not supported; we must not silently change the semantics of a model.)
* **Keep export code simple.** We don't want frontends to have to write/test N different export modes, one for each "version" of ONNX. Exports should be written once, for a particular version of ONNX; cross-version interoperability code should be shared amongst all frameworks. (Note that there is no corresponding principle for importers.)
* **Validate offline.** A backend should be able to validate the code it is given its knowledge of some fixed version of the ONNX spec, and not have to query some remote service/URL in order to get "updated" information about a specification. (This rules out SGML-style designs, where the DTD is specified via a remote URL.)

**The proposal.**

* Every operator (as seen in https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md) is associated with a permanent, unique identifier called the **operator identifier**. To make it clear that we are referring to the unique identifier, all operator identifiers will be prefixed with an underscore (this signifier can be bikeshed.) The operator semantics for an given operator identifier never changes; operator identifiers are *immutable.* For example, suppose that addition with a broadcast flag as operator identifier `_Add`. If you want a new addition operator which implicitly broadcasted, you must define a new operator with a new unique identifier, e.g., `_BroadcastingAdd`. 

* The `op_type` field (hitherto referred to as the **operator name**) does NOT share a namespace with operator identifiers. Rather, operator names are mapped to operator identifiers, via the mechanism specified below. So, for example, in one version of ONNX, the operator name `Add` would map to `_BroadcastingAdd`.
  * Option: a ModelProto can short circuit the namespace mechanism by writing the operator identifier, e.g., `_BroadcastingAdd` in the proto. This mode of use would be discouraged, but might give an escape hatch for when we deprecate operators without providing replacements in the new form, and also provide a way for backends to opt out of implementing the namespace mechanism (by "pre-translating" names into identifier).

* Every ONNX ModelProto is associated with **ONNX operator namespace version**, which specifies a mapping from operator names to operator identifiers. The mappings for every version is codified into the standard, and will be distributed in protobuf form for easy consumption by backends. Every increment of the ONNX operator library version represents a backwards *incompatible* change to the namespace mapping. For example, if operator version 1 has `Add` mapped to `_Add`, and we would like to change ONNX to have implicit broadcasting, we would increase the operator version to 2, allowing us to specify a new mapping of `Add` to `_BroadcastingAdd`.
  * Although the naive way to represent these mappings is imply a mapping over namespace version to (mapping from operator name to operator id), these mappings can be compressed by instead recording only diffs. These diffs have a semantic meaning: they're the changelog for the operator library!
  * Another possibility is to make the ONNX operator library version semver. In this case, whenever a new operator is added to ONNX (backwards compatible!), we would bump the minor version of the operator library version.  On the other hand, this might be too much administrative work, so I'm not entirely sure if we should bother.

* A frontend which exports to ONNX picks an ONNX operator namespace version it wants to support (hopefully the latest version of ONNX available to it), and implements its exporters to produce operators according to these names.   Now there are two possibilities for what it records:
  * A less interoperable frontend simply reports the operator namespace version it is targeting, and calls it a day. The downside is any importer which is built against an earlier version of ONNX will always fail to interpret the model (even if the subset of operators actually used in the model are ones it knows.)
  * A more interoperable frontend finds the *earliest* operator namespace version whose mapping agrees with the subset of the mapping that was used for operators, using the normative protobuf information that is part of the ONNX spec. It then exports records *that* version in the protobuf.

* A backend which imports ONNX implements an importer per operator identifier. It also consumes normative protobuf describing all of the mappings as per the ONNX spec, and uses this to resolve operator names (recorded in the protobuf) to operator identifiers, as per the operator namespace version recorded in the protobuf. Even if a backend advertises support for an ONNX namespace version, it is allowed to fail on certain operators, stating that it doesn't support them.

* In the ONNX library, we provide converters that translate operators from old namespace versions to new namespace versions. It will be considered good style to add a converter translating a deprecated operator into the recommended new form.

**How does this proposal fulfill our goals?**

* To make a BC breaking change to an operator, you create a completely new operator with a fresh operator identifier, and then you bump the operator namespace version, swapping the old name to the new name.

* Old models do not break. Even if mappings in the operator namespace change, a ModelProto will always specify the exact semantics of every operator via its operator namespace version. Assuming importers only increase the set of operator identifiers they support, old models are supported indefinitely.

* Export code is simple: it need only target a specific operator namespace version. A small amount of (generic) code (with some more complicated, but standardized, metadata) can allow exporters to export models which are compatible with backends that target earlier versions of ONNX, if no operators which had BC-breaking changes are used. Obviously, it is impossible to export a model with a new operator if an importer is not programmed to support it; however, conversions in the ONNX library may help users get the models they want into the form they need for the backends they want to target.

* Importers can validate ONNX models based on their knowledge of the spec (because they are not expected to be future compatible; that is the exporter's job), and thus don't have to query a remote service to figure out what to do.
